**New York**, also called **New York State**,[[b]](#cite_note-11) is a [state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") located in the [northeastern United States](/wiki/Northeastern_United_States "Northeastern United States"). Bordering the region of [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") to its east, the country of [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") to the north, and [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania") and [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey") to the south, its territory extends into both the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") and the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes").[[c]](#cite_note-12) New York is the [fourth-most populous state in the United States](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_population "List of U.S. states and territories by population"), with over 20 million residents,[[10]](#cite_note-NewYorkQuickFacts-13) and the [27th-largest state by area](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_area "List of U.S. states and territories by area"), with a total area of 54,556 square miles (141,300 km2).[[7]](#cite_note-area-7)

New York has [a varied geography](/wiki/Geography_of_New_York_(state) "Geography of New York (state)"). The southeastern part of the state, known as [Downstate](/wiki/Downstate_New_York "Downstate New York"), encompasses [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), the [most populous city in the United States](/wiki/List_of_U.S._cities_by_population "List of U.S. cities by population"); [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island"), with approximately 40% of the state's population, the nation's most populous island; and the cities, suburbs, and wealthy enclaves of the lower [Hudson Valley](/wiki/Hudson_Valley "Hudson Valley"). These areas are the center of the expansive [New York metropolitan area](/wiki/New_York_metropolitan_area "New York metropolitan area")[[11]](#cite_note-14)[[12]](#cite_note-15) and account for approximately two-thirds of the state's population. The larger [Upstate](/wiki/Upstate_New_York "Upstate New York") area spreads from the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes") to [Lake Champlain](/wiki/Lake_Champlain "Lake Champlain") and includes the [Adirondack Mountains](/wiki/Adirondack_Mountains "Adirondack Mountains") and the [Catskill Mountains](/wiki/Catskill_Mountains "Catskill Mountains") (part of the wider [Appalachian Mountains](/wiki/Appalachian_Mountains "Appalachian Mountains")). The east–west [Mohawk River](/wiki/Mohawk_River "Mohawk River") Valley bisects the more mountainous regions of Upstate and flows into the north–south [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River") valley near the [state capital](/wiki/List_of_U.S._state_capitals "List of U.S. state capitals") of [Albany](/wiki/Albany,_New_York "Albany, New York"). [Western New York](/wiki/Western_New_York "Western New York"), which is sometimes considered to be a separate region from the Upstate, is home to the cities of [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York") and [Rochester](/wiki/Rochester,_New_York "Rochester, New York"), and is part of the [Great Lakes region](/wiki/Great_Lakes_region "Great Lakes region"), bordering [Lake Ontario](/wiki/Lake_Ontario "Lake Ontario") and [Lake Erie](/wiki/Lake_Erie "Lake Erie"). [Central New York](/wiki/Central_New_York "Central New York") is anchored by the city of [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_New_York "Syracuse, New York"); between the central and western parts of the state, New York is prominently featured by the [Finger Lakes](/wiki/Finger_Lakes "Finger Lakes"), a popular tourist destination. To the south, along the state border with Pennsylvania, the [Southern Tier](/wiki/Southern_Tier "Southern Tier") sits atop the [Allegheny Plateau](/wiki/Allegheny_Plateau "Allegheny Plateau"), representing some of the northernmost reaches of [Appalachia](/wiki/Appalachia "Appalachia").

New York was one of the original [Thirteen Colonies](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies") that went on to form the United States. The area of present-day New York had been inhabited by tribes of the [Algonquians](/wiki/Algonquian_peoples "Algonquian peoples") and the [Iroquois](/wiki/Iroquois "Iroquois") Confederacy [Native Americans](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") for several thousand years by the time the earliest Europeans arrived.[[13]](#cite_note-Zumbusch-2014-16) Stemming from [Henry Hudson](/wiki/Henry_Hudson "Henry Hudson")'s expedition in 1609,[[14]](#cite_note-17) the [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_people "Dutch people") established the multiethnic colony of [New Netherland](/wiki/New_Netherland "New Netherland") in 1621. [England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England") seized the colony from the Dutch in 1664, renaming it the [Province of New York](/wiki/Province_of_New_York "Province of New York").[[15]](#cite_note-18) Upstate New York was fought over between the British and the French during the [French and Indian War](/wiki/French_and_Indian_War "French and Indian War") from 1754 to 1763. During the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), a group of colonists eventually succeeded in establishing independence, and the state ratified the then new [United States Constitution](/wiki/United_States_Constitution "United States Constitution") in 1788. From the early 19th century, New York's development of its interior, beginning with the construction of the [Erie Canal](/wiki/Erie_Canal "Erie Canal"), gave it incomparable advantages over other regions of the United States.[[16]](#cite_note-Roberts-19) The state built its political, cultural, and economic ascendancy over the next century, earning it the nickname of the "[Empire State](/wiki/Empire_State "Empire State")". Although [deindustrialization](/wiki/Rust_Belt "Rust Belt") eroded a portion of the state's economy in the second half of the 20th century, New York in the 21st century continues to be considered as a global node of [creativity](/wiki/Innovation "Innovation") and [entrepreneurship](/wiki/Entrepreneurship "Entrepreneurship"),[[17]](#cite_note-VentureCapitalNY1-20) [social tolerance](/wiki/Social_tolerance "Social tolerance"),[[18]](#cite_note-SocialToleranceNY1-21) and [environmental sustainability](/wiki/Environmental_sustainability "Environmental sustainability").[[19]](#cite_note-EnvironmentalSustainabilityNY1-22)[[20]](#cite_note-EnvironmentalSustainabilityNY2-23)

The state attracts visitors from all over the globe, with the highest count of any U.S. state in 2022.[[21]](#cite_note-24) Many of its landmarks are well known, including four of the world's ten most-visited tourist attractions in 2013: [Times Square](/wiki/Times_Square "Times Square"), [Central Park](/wiki/Central_Park "Central Park"), [Niagara Falls](/wiki/Niagara_Falls "Niagara Falls"), and [Grand Central Terminal](/wiki/Grand_Central_Terminal "Grand Central Terminal").[[22]](#cite_note-Ann_Shields-25) New York is home to [approximately 200 colleges and universities](/wiki/List_of_colleges_and_universities_in_New_York_(state) "List of colleges and universities in New York (state)"), including [Ivy League](/wiki/Ivy_League "Ivy League") members [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University") and [Cornell University](/wiki/Cornell_University "Cornell University"), and the expansive [State University of New York](/wiki/State_University_of_New_York "State University of New York"), which is among the largest university systems in the nation.[[23]](#cite_note-usnews-26)[[24]](#cite_note-27)[[25]](#cite_note-28)[[26]](#cite_note-suny-29) New York City is home to the [headquarters of the United Nations](/wiki/Headquarters_of_the_United_Nations "Headquarters of the United Nations"),[[27]](#cite_note-30) and it is sometimes described as the world's most important city,[[28]](#cite_note-NYCWorld'sMostImportantCity-31)[[29]](#cite_note-NYCWorld'sMostImportantCity2-32) the [cultural](/wiki/Culture_of_New_York_City "Culture of New York City"),[[30]](#cite_note-33)[[31]](#cite_note-culture4-34) [financial](/wiki/Wall_Street "Wall Street"),[[32]](#cite_note-WorldEconomicAndFinancialSuperCenter-35)[[33]](#cite_note-NYCDominantFinancialCenter-36)[[34]](#cite_note-NewYorkFinancialCapitalWorld-37) and [media](/wiki/Media_in_New_York_City "Media in New York City") epicenter,[[35]](#cite_note-38)[[36]](#cite_note-39) and the capital of the world.[[37]](#cite_note-NYCTheCapitaloftheWorld-40)[[38]](#cite_note-NewYorkCapitaloftheWorld2-41)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of New York (state)](/wiki/History_of_New_York_(state) "History of New York (state)")

### Native American history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Native American history")]

[![Map of New York showing Algonquian tribes in the eastern and southern portions and Iroquoian tribes to the western and northern portions.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Early_Localization_Native_Americans_NY.svg/250px-Early_Localization_Native_Americans_NY.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Early_Localization_Native_Americans_NY.svg)

Prior to European settlement, New York was dominated by [Iroquoian](/wiki/Iroquoian_languages "Iroquoian languages") (purple) and [Algonquian](/wiki/Algonquian_peoples "Algonquian peoples") (red) [tribes](/wiki/Tribe_(Native_American) "Tribe (Native American)").

The [Native American](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") tribes in what is now New York were predominantly [Iroquois](/wiki/Iroquois "Iroquois") and [Algonquian](/wiki/Algonquian_peoples "Algonquian peoples").[[13]](#cite_note-Zumbusch-2014-16) [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island") was divided roughly in half between the Algonquian [Wampanoag](/wiki/Wampanoag "Wampanoag") and [Lenape](/wiki/Lenape "Lenape") peoples. The Lenape also controlled most of the region surrounding [New York Harbor](/wiki/New_York_Harbor "New York Harbor").[[39]](#cite_note-42) North of the Lenape was a third Algonquian nation, the [Mohicans](/wiki/Mohican "Mohican"). Starting north of them, from east to west, were two Iroquoian nations: the [Mohawk](/wiki/Mohawk_people "Mohawk people")—part of the original Iroquois Five Nations, and the [Petun](/wiki/Petun "Petun"). South of them, divided roughly along [Appalachia](/wiki/Appalachia "Appalachia"), were the [Susquehannock](/wiki/Susquehannock "Susquehannock") and the [Erie](/wiki/Erie_(tribe) "Erie (tribe)").[[40]](#cite_note-43)[[41]](#cite_note-44)[[42]](#cite_note-45)[[43]](#cite_note-46)

Many of the Wampanoag and Mohican peoples were caught up in [King Philip's War](/wiki/King_Philip%27s_War "King Philip's War"), a joint effort of many [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") tribes to push Europeans off their land. After the death of their leader, Chief Philip [Metacomet](/wiki/Metacomet "Metacomet"), most of those peoples fled inland, splitting into the [Abenaki](/wiki/Abenaki "Abenaki") and the [Schaghticoke](/wiki/Schaghticoke_people "Schaghticoke people"). Many of the Mohicans remained in the region until the 1800s,[[44]](#cite_note-47) however, a small group known as the Ouabano migrated southwest into [West Virginia](/wiki/Protohistory_of_West_Virginia "Protohistory of West Virginia") at an earlier time. They may have merged with the [Shawnee](/wiki/Shawnee "Shawnee").[[45]](#cite_note-48)[[46]](#cite_note-49)

The Mohawk and Susquehannock were the most [militaristic](/wiki/Militarism "Militarism"). Trying to corner trade with the Europeans, they targeted other tribes. The Mohawk were also known for refusing white settlement on their land and discriminating against any of their people who converted to [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity").[[47]](#cite_note-50) They posed a major threat to the Abenaki and Mohicans, while the Susquehannock briefly conquered the Lenape in the 1600s. The most devastating event of the century, however, was the [Beaver Wars](/wiki/Beaver_Wars "Beaver Wars").

From approximately 1640–1680, the Iroquois peoples waged campaigns which extended from modern-day Michigan to Virginia against Algonquian and Siouan tribes, as well as each other. The aim was to control more land for animal [trapping](/wiki/Trapping "Trapping"),[[48]](#cite_note-51) a career most natives had turned to in hopes of trading with whites first. This completely changed the ethnography of the region, and most large game was hunted out before whites ever fully explored the land. Still, afterward, the Iroquois Confederacy offered shelter to refugees of the [Mascouten](/wiki/Mascouten "Mascouten"), Erie, [Chonnonton](/wiki/Neutral_Nation "Neutral Nation"), [Tutelo](/wiki/Tutelo "Tutelo"), [Saponi](/wiki/Saponi "Saponi"), and [Tuscarora](/wiki/Tuscarora_people "Tuscarora people") nations. The Tuscarora became the sixth nation of the Iroquois in around 1720.[[49]](#cite_note-52)

In the 1700s, Iroquoian peoples would take in the remaining Susquehannock of Pennsylvania after they were decimated in the [French and Indian War](/wiki/French-Indian_War "French-Indian War").[[50]](#cite_note-53) Most of these other groups assimilated and eventually ceased to exist as separate tribes. Then, after the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution"), a large group of Seneca split off and returned to Ohio, becoming known as the [Mingo](/wiki/Mingo "Mingo") Seneca. The current Six Nations of the Iroquois Confederacy include the Seneca, Cayuga, Onondaga, Oneida, Tuscarora and Mohawk. The Iroquois fought for both sides during the [Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"); afterwards many pro-British Iroquois migrated to Canada. Today, the Iroquois still live in several enclaves across New York and Ontario.[[51]](#cite_note-54)[[52]](#cite_note-55)[[53]](#cite_note-56)[[54]](#cite_note-57)

Meanwhile, the Lenape formed a close relationship with [William Penn](/wiki/William_Penn "William Penn"). However, upon Penn's death, his sons managed to take over much of their lands and banish them to Ohio.[[55]](#cite_note-58) When the U.S. drafted the [Indian Removal Act](/wiki/Indian_Removal_Act "Indian Removal Act"), the Lenape were further moved to Missouri, whereas their cousins, the Mohicans, were sent to Wisconsin.

Also, in 1778, the United States relocated the [Nanticoke](/wiki/Nanticoke_people "Nanticoke people") from the [Delmarva Peninsula](/wiki/Delmarva_Peninsula "Delmarva Peninsula") to the former Iroquois lands south of Lake Ontario, though they did not stay long. Mostly, they chose to migrate into Canada and merge with the Iroquois, although some moved west and merged with the Lenape.[[56]](#cite_note-59)

### 16th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 16th century")]

In 1524, [Giovanni da Verrazzano](/wiki/Giovanni_da_Verrazzano "Giovanni da Verrazzano"), an [Italian](/wiki/Italian_people "Italian people") explorer in the service of the [French crown](/wiki/French_crown "French crown"), explored the [Atlantic](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") coast of [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America") between the [Carolinas](/wiki/Carolinas "Carolinas") and [Newfoundland](/wiki/Newfoundland "Newfoundland"), including [New York Harbor](/wiki/New_York_Harbor "New York Harbor") and [Narragansett Bay](/wiki/Narragansett_Bay "Narragansett Bay"). On April 17, 1524, Verrazzano entered [New York Bay](/wiki/New_York_Bay "New York Bay"),[[57]](#cite_note-60)[[58]](#cite_note-61) by way of the strait now called [the Narrows](/wiki/The_Narrows "The Narrows") into the northern bay which he named Santa Margherita, in honor of the King of France's [sister](/wiki/Marguerite_de_Navarre "Marguerite de Navarre"). Verrazzano described it as "a vast coastline with a deep delta in which every kind of ship could pass" and he adds: "that it extends inland for a league and opens up to form a beautiful lake. This vast sheet of water swarmed with native boats." He landed on the tip of Manhattan and possibly on the furthest point of Long Island. Verrazzano's stay was interrupted by a storm which pushed him north towards [Martha's Vineyard](/wiki/Martha%27s_Vineyard "Martha's Vineyard").[[59]](#cite_note-62)

In 1540, French traders from New France built a [chateau](/wiki/Chateau "Chateau") on [Castle Island](/wiki/Castle_Island_(New_York) "Castle Island (New York)"), within present-day Albany; it was abandoned the following year due to flooding. In 1614, the Dutch, under the command of Hendrick Corstiaensen, rebuilt the French chateau, which they called [Fort Nassau](/wiki/Fort_Nassau_(North_River) "Fort Nassau (North River)").[[60]](#cite_note-NewNetherland-63) Fort Nassau was the first Dutch settlement in North America, and was located along the Hudson River, also within present-day Albany. The small fort served as a trading post and warehouse. Located on the Hudson River flood plain, the rudimentary fort was washed away by flooding in 1617,[[61]](#cite_note-64) and abandoned for good after [Fort Orange (New Netherland)](/wiki/Fort_Orange "Fort Orange") was built nearby in 1623.[[62]](#cite_note-Chronicles-65)

### 17th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 17th century")]

Main articles: [New Netherland](/wiki/New_Netherland "New Netherland"), [Province of New York](/wiki/Province_of_New_York "Province of New York"), and [Dominion of New England](/wiki/Dominion_of_New_England "Dominion of New England")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Castelloplan.jpg/250px-Castelloplan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Castelloplan.jpg)

A 1660 map illustration of [New Amsterdam](/wiki/New_Amsterdam "New Amsterdam"), present-day [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan")

Henry Hudson's 1609 voyage marked the beginning of European involvement in the area. Sailing for the [Dutch East India Company](/wiki/Dutch_East_India_Company "Dutch East India Company") and looking for a passage to Asia, he entered the [Upper New York Bay](/wiki/Upper_New_York_Bay "Upper New York Bay") on September 11 of that year.[[63]](#cite_note-66) Word of his findings encouraged Dutch merchants to explore the coast in search of profitable fur trading with local Native American tribes.

During the 17th century, Dutch [trading posts](/wiki/Trading_post "Trading post") established for the trade of pelts from the Lenape, Iroquois, and other tribes were founded in the colony of [New Netherland](/wiki/New_Netherland "New Netherland"). The first of these trading posts were Fort Nassau (1614, near present-day [Albany](/wiki/Albany,_New_York "Albany, New York"));[[60]](#cite_note-NewNetherland-63) Fort Orange (1624, on the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River") just south of the current city of Albany and created to replace Fort Nassau), developing into settlement [Beverwijck](/wiki/Beverwijck "Beverwijck") (1647), and into what became Albany; [Fort Amsterdam](/wiki/Fort_Amsterdam "Fort Amsterdam") (1625, to develop into the town [New Amsterdam](/wiki/New_Amsterdam "New Amsterdam"), which is present-day New York City); and Esopus (1653, now [Kingston](/wiki/Kingston,_New_York "Kingston, New York")). The success of the [patroonship](/wiki/Patroon "Patroon") of [Rensselaerswyck](/wiki/Manor_of_Rensselaerswyck "Manor of Rensselaerswyck") (1630), which surrounded Albany and lasted until the mid-19th century, was also a key factor in the early success of the colony. The English captured the colony during the [Second Anglo-Dutch War](/wiki/Second_Anglo-Dutch_War "Second Anglo-Dutch War") and governed it as the [Province of New York](/wiki/Province_of_New_York "Province of New York"). The city of New York was recaptured by the Dutch in 1673 during the [Third Anglo-Dutch War](/wiki/Third_Anglo-Dutch_War "Third Anglo-Dutch War") (1672–1674) and renamed **New Orange**. It was returned to the English under the terms of the [Treaty of Westminster](/wiki/Treaty_of_Westminster_(1674) "Treaty of Westminster (1674)") a year later.[[64]](#cite_note-67)

### 18th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 18th century")]

#### French and Indian War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: French and Indian War")]

Upstate New York was a battleground between Britain and France during the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War"). Notable battles include the battles at [Fort William Henry](/wiki/Fort_William_Henry "Fort William Henry") and [Fort Ticonderoga](/wiki/Fort_Ticonderoga "Fort Ticonderoga").
At the outbreak of hostilities in 1754, represtatives from the 13 colonies met in New York for the [Albany Congress](/wiki/Albany_Congress "Albany Congress") to discuss joint defense against the French. It was one of the first times the colonies debated joining together.

#### American Revolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: American Revolution")]

Further information: [Saratoga campaign](/wiki/Saratoga_campaign "Saratoga campaign")

[![A painting of British general John Burgoyne and his men surrendering at Saratoga, 1777](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Surrender_of_General_Burgoyne.jpg/250px-Surrender_of_General_Burgoyne.jpg)](/wiki/File:Surrender_of_General_Burgoyne.jpg)

Illustration of [British](/wiki/British_Army_during_the_American_Revolutionary_War "British Army during the American Revolutionary War") general [John Burgoyne](/wiki/John_Burgoyne "John Burgoyne") surrendering at [Saratoga](/wiki/Saratoga_campaign "Saratoga campaign") on October 17, 1777

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/1800LowsNYstate.jpeg/250px-1800LowsNYstate.jpeg)](/wiki/File:1800LowsNYstate.jpeg)

An 1800 map of New York from *[Low's Encyclopaedia](/wiki/Low%27s_Encyclopaedia "Low's Encyclopaedia")*

The [Sons of Liberty](/wiki/Sons_of_Liberty "Sons of Liberty") were organized in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") during the 1760s, largely in response to the oppressive [Stamp Act](/wiki/Stamp_Act_1765 "Stamp Act 1765") passed by the [British Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") in 1765.[[65]](#cite_note-68) The [Stamp Act Congress](/wiki/Stamp_Act_Congress "Stamp Act Congress") met in the city on October 19 of that year, composed of representatives from across the [Thirteen Colonies](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies") who set the stage for the [Continental Congress](/wiki/Continental_Congress "Continental Congress") to follow. The Stamp Act Congress resulted in the [Declaration of Rights and Grievances](/wiki/Declaration_of_Rights_and_Grievances "Declaration of Rights and Grievances"), which was the first written expression by representatives of the Americans of many of the rights and complaints later expressed in the [United States Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence"). This included the right to [representative government](/wiki/No_taxation_without_representation "No taxation without representation"). At the same time, given strong commercial, personal and sentimental links to [Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain"), many New York residents were [Loyalists](/wiki/Loyalist "Loyalist"). The [Capture of Fort Ticonderoga](/wiki/Capture_of_Fort_Ticonderoga "Capture of Fort Ticonderoga") provided the [cannon and gunpowder](/wiki/Fortification_of_Dorchester_Heights "Fortification of Dorchester Heights") necessary to force a [British](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") withdrawal from the [siege of Boston](/wiki/Siege_of_Boston "Siege of Boston") in 1775.

New York was the only colony [not to vote for independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence#Draft_and_adoption "United States Declaration of Independence"), as the delegates were not authorized to do so. New York then endorsed the [Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence") on July 9, 1776.[[66]](#cite_note-69) The [New York State Constitution](/wiki/New_York_Constitution "New York Constitution") was framed by a [convention](/wiki/Constitutional_convention_(political_meeting) "Constitutional convention (political meeting)") which assembled at [White Plains](/wiki/White_Plains,_New_York "White Plains, New York") on July 10, 1776, and after repeated adjournments and changes of location, finished its work at [Kingston](/wiki/Kingston,_New_York "Kingston, New York") on Sunday evening, April 20, 1777, when the [new constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_New_York,_1777 "Constitution of New York, 1777") drafted by [John Jay](/wiki/John_Jay "John Jay") was adopted with but one dissenting vote. It was not submitted to the people for ratification. On July 30, 1777, [George Clinton](/wiki/George_Clinton_(vice_president) "George Clinton (vice president)") was inaugurated as the first [Governor of New York](/wiki/Governor_of_New_York "Governor of New York") at Kingston.[[67]](#cite_note-70)

Approximately a third of the battles of the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War") took place in New York; the first major one and largest of the entire war was the [Battle of Long Island](/wiki/Battle_of_Long_Island "Battle of Long Island"), also known as the Battle of Brooklyn, in August 1776. After their victory, the British occupied present-day New York City, making it their military and political base of operations in North America for the duration of the conflict, and consequently the focus of General [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington")'s [intelligence network](/wiki/Intelligence_operations_in_the_American_Revolutionary_War "Intelligence operations in the American Revolutionary War"). On the notorious British [prison ships](/wiki/HMS_Jersey_(1736) "HMS Jersey (1736)") of [Wallabout Bay](/wiki/Wallabout_Bay "Wallabout Bay"), more American combatants died than were killed in combat in every battle of the war combined. Both sides of combatants lost more soldiers to disease than to outright wounds. The first of two major British armies were captured by the [Continental Army](/wiki/Continental_Army "Continental Army") at the [Battle of Saratoga](/wiki/Battles_of_Saratoga "Battles of Saratoga") in 1777,[[68]](#cite_note-History-2020-71) a success that influenced [France](/wiki/France "France") to ally with the revolutionaries; the state constitution was enacted in 1777. New York became the 11th state to ratify the [United States Constitution](/wiki/United_States_Constitution "United States Constitution"), on July 26, 1788.

In an attempt to retain their [sovereignty](/wiki/Sovereign_nation "Sovereign nation") and remain an independent nation positioned between the new United States and [British North America](/wiki/British_America "British America"), four of the [Iroquois Nations](/wiki/Iroquois "Iroquois") fought on the side of the British; only the [Oneida](/wiki/Oneida_people "Oneida people") and their dependents, the Tuscarora, allied themselves with the Americans.[[69]](#cite_note-72) In retaliation for attacks on the frontier led by [Joseph Brant](/wiki/Joseph_Brant "Joseph Brant") and Loyalist [Mohawk forces](/wiki/Mohawk_people "Mohawk people"), the [Sullivan Expedition](/wiki/Sullivan_Expedition "Sullivan Expedition") of 1779 destroyed nearly 50 Iroquois villages, adjacent croplands and winter stores, forcing many refugees to British-held Niagara.[[70]](#cite_note-73)

As allies of the British, the Iroquois were forced out of New York, although they had not been part of treaty negotiations. They resettled in Canada after the war and were given land grants by the Crown. In the treaty settlement, the British ceded most Indian lands to the new United States. Because New York made a treaty with the Iroquois without getting Congressional approval, some of the land purchases have been subject to land claim suits since the late 20th century by the federally recognized tribes. New York put up more than 5 million acres (20,000 km2) of former Iroquois territory for sale in the years after the Revolutionary War, leading to rapid development in Upstate New York.[[71]](#cite_note-74) As per the [Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1783) "Treaty of Paris (1783)"), the last vestige of British authority in the former [Thirteen Colonies](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies")—their troops in New York City—departed in 1783, which was long afterward celebrated as [Evacuation Day](/wiki/Evacuation_Day_(New_York) "Evacuation Day (New York)").[[72]](#cite_note-75)

#### Confederation period and 1790s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Confederation period and 1790s")]

New York City was the national capital under the [Articles of Confederation and Perpetual Union](/wiki/Articles_of_Confederation_and_Perpetual_Union "Articles of Confederation and Perpetual Union"), the first national government. That organization was found to be inadequate, and prominent New Yorker [Alexander Hamilton](/wiki/Alexander_Hamilton "Alexander Hamilton") advocated for a new government that would include an executive, national courts, and the power to tax. Hamilton led the [Annapolis Convention (1786)](/wiki/Annapolis_Convention_(1786) "Annapolis Convention (1786)") that called for the [Philadelphia Convention](/wiki/Philadelphia_Convention "Philadelphia Convention"), which drafted the United States Constitution, in which he also took part. The new government was to be a strong [federal](/wiki/Federation "Federation") national government to replace the relatively weaker [confederation](/wiki/Confederation "Confederation") of individual states. Following heated debate, which included the publication of *[The Federalist Papers](/wiki/The_Federalist_Papers "The Federalist Papers")* as a series of installments in New York City newspapers, New York was the 11th state to ratify the [United States Constitution](/wiki/United_States_Constitution "United States Constitution"), on July 26, 1788.[[73]](#cite_note-76)

New York City remained the national capital under the new constitution until 1790 when it was moved to [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") until 1800, when it was relocated to its current location in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.")[[74]](#cite_note-77) and was the site of the inauguration of President George Washington,[[75]](#cite_note-78) In the first session of the [Supreme Court of the United States](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States"), the [United States Bill of Rights](/wiki/United_States_Bill_of_Rights "United States Bill of Rights") were drafted.

### 19th and 20th centuries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 19th and 20th centuries")]

Further information: [New York in the American Civil War](/wiki/New_York_in_the_American_Civil_War "New York in the American Civil War")

[![A painting of the Erie Canal, depicted in 1839.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Lockport_bartlett_color_crop.jpg/250px-Lockport_bartlett_color_crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lockport_bartlett_color_crop.jpg)

An illustration of the [Erie Canal](/wiki/Erie_Canal "Erie Canal") at [Lockport](/wiki/Lockport,_New_York "Lockport, New York") in 1839

Transportation in [Western New York](/wiki/Western_New_York "Western New York") was by expensive wagons on muddy roads before canals opened up the rich farmlands to long-distance traffic. Governor [DeWitt Clinton](/wiki/DeWitt_Clinton "DeWitt Clinton") promoted the [Erie Canal](/wiki/Erie_Canal "Erie Canal"), which connected [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") to the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes") by the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River"), the new canal, and the rivers and lakes. Work commenced in 1817, and the [Erie Canal](/wiki/Erie_Canal "Erie Canal") opened eight years later, in 1825. [Packet boats](/wiki/Packet_boat "Packet boat") pulled by horses on tow paths traveled slowly over the canal carrying passengers and freight.[[76]](#cite_note-ErieCanal-79) Farm products came in from the [Midwest](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States"), and finished manufactured goods moved west. It was an engineering marvel which opened up vast areas of New York to commerce and settlement. It enabled Great Lakes port cities such as [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York") and [Rochester](/wiki/Rochester,_New_York "Rochester, New York") to grow and prosper. It also connected the burgeoning agricultural production of the Midwest and shipping on the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes"), with the port of New York City. Improving transportation, it enabled additional population migration to territories west of New York. After 1850, railroads largely replaced the canal.[[77]](#cite_note-80)

The connectivity offered by the canal, and subsequently the railroads, led to an economic boom across the entire state through the 1950s. Major corporations that got their start in New York during this time include [American Express](/wiki/American_Express "American Express"), [AT&T](/wiki/AT%26T_Corporation "AT&T Corporation"), [Bristol Myers Squibb](/wiki/Bristol_Myers_Squibb "Bristol Myers Squibb"), [Carrier](/wiki/Carrier_Global "Carrier Global"), [Chase](/wiki/Chase_Bank "Chase Bank"), [General Electric](/wiki/General_Electric "General Electric"), [Goldman Sachs](/wiki/Goldman_Sachs "Goldman Sachs"), [IBM](/wiki/IBM "IBM"), [Kodak](/wiki/Eastman_Kodak "Eastman Kodak"), [Macy's](/wiki/Macy%27s "Macy's"), [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC"), [Pfizer](/wiki/Pfizer "Pfizer"), [Random House](/wiki/Random_House "Random House"), [RCA](/wiki/RCA_Corporation "RCA Corporation"), [Tiffany & Co.](/wiki/Tiffany_%26_Co. "Tiffany & Co."), [Wells Fargo](/wiki/Wells_Fargo "Wells Fargo"), [Western Union](/wiki/Western_Union "Western Union"), and [Xerox](/wiki/Xerox "Xerox").

New York City was a major [ocean port](/wiki/Port "Port") and had extensive traffic importing cotton from the [South](/wiki/American_South "American South") and exporting manufacturing goods. Nearly half of the state's exports were related to cotton. Southern cotton factors, planters and bankers visited so often that they had favorite hotels.[[78]](#cite_note-81) At the same time, activism for [abolitionism](/wiki/Abolitionism "Abolitionism") was strong upstate, where some communities provided stops on the [Underground Railroad](/wiki/Underground_Railroad "Underground Railroad"). Upstate, and New York City, gave strong support for the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), in terms of finances, volunteer soldiers, and supplies. The state provided more than 370,000 soldiers to the [Union](/wiki/Union_(American_Civil_War) "Union (American Civil War)") armies. Over 53,000 New Yorkers died in service, roughly one of every seven who served. However, Irish draft riots in 1862 were a significant embarrassment.[[79]](#cite_note-82)[[80]](#cite_note-83)

#### Immigration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Immigration")]

Further information: [Ellis Island](/wiki/Ellis_Island "Ellis Island")

Scenes at the Immigration Depot and a nearby dock on [Ellis Island](/wiki/Ellis_Island "Ellis Island") in May 1906

Since the early 19th century, New York City has been the largest [port of entry](/wiki/Port_of_entry "Port of entry") for [legal immigration into the United States](/wiki/Immigration_to_the_United_States "Immigration to the United States"). In the United States, the [federal government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States") did not assume direct jurisdiction for immigration until 1890. Prior to this time, the matter was delegated to the individual states, then via contract between the states and the federal government. Most immigrants to New York would disembark at the bustling docks along the Hudson and [East Rivers](/wiki/East_River "East River"), in the eventual [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan"). On May 4, 1847, the [New York State Legislature](/wiki/New_York_State_Legislature "New York State Legislature") created the Board of Commissioners of Immigration to regulate immigration.[[81]](#cite_note-84)

The first permanent immigration depot in New York was established in 1855 at [Castle Garden](/wiki/Castle_Clinton "Castle Clinton"), a converted [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812") era fort located within what is now [Battery Park](/wiki/Battery_Park "Battery Park"), at the tip of Lower Manhattan. The first immigrants to arrive at the new depot were aboard three ships that had just been released from [quarantine](/wiki/Quarantine "Quarantine"). Castle Garden served as New York's immigrant depot until it closed on April 18, 1890, when the federal government assumed control over immigration. During that period, more than eight million immigrants passed through its doors (two of every three U.S. immigrants).[[82]](#cite_note-85)

When the federal government assumed control, it established the [Bureau of Immigration](/wiki/Immigration_and_Naturalization_Service "Immigration and Naturalization Service"), which chose the three-acre (1.2 ha) Ellis Island in [Upper New York Harbor](/wiki/Upper_New_York_Bay "Upper New York Bay") for an entry depot. Already federally controlled, the island had served as an ammunition depot. It was chosen due its relative isolation with proximity to New York City and the rail lines of [Jersey City, New Jersey](/wiki/Jersey_City,_New_Jersey "Jersey City, New Jersey"), via a short ferry ride. While the island was being developed and expanded via [land reclamation](/wiki/Land_reclamation "Land reclamation"), the federal government operated a temporary depot at the Barge Office at the Battery.[[83]](#cite_note-86)

Ellis Island opened on January 1, 1892, and operated as a central immigration center until the [National Origins Act](/wiki/Immigration_Act_of_1924 "Immigration Act of 1924") was passed in 1924, reducing immigration. After that date, the only immigrants to pass through were [displaced persons](/wiki/Displaced_persons "Displaced persons") or war [refugees](/wiki/Refugees "Refugees"). The island ceased all immigration processing on November 12, 1954, when the last person detained on the island, [Norwegian](/wiki/Norwegians "Norwegians") seaman Arne Peterssen, was released. He had overstayed his shore leave and left on the 10:15 a.m. Manhattan-bound ferry to return to his ship.

More than 12 million immigrants passed through Ellis Island between 1892 and 1954. More than 100 million Americans across the United States can trace their [ancestry](/wiki/Ancestry "Ancestry") to these immigrants. Ellis Island was the subject of a contentious and long-running border and jurisdictional dispute between the State of New York and the State of New Jersey, as both claimed it. The issue was officially settled in 1998 by the [U.S. Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States") which ruled that the original 3.3-acre (1.3 ha) island was New York state territory and that the balance of the 27.5 acres (11 ha) added after 1834 by landfill was in New Jersey.[[84]](#cite_note-87) In May 1964, Ellis Island was added to the [National Park Service](/wiki/National_Park_Service "National Park Service") by President [Lyndon B. Johnson](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson "Lyndon B. Johnson") and is still owned by the federal government as part of the [Statue of Liberty National Monument](/wiki/Statue_of_Liberty_National_Monument "Statue of Liberty National Monument"). In 1990, Ellis Island was opened to the public as a museum of immigration.[[85]](#cite_note-88)

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 21st century")]

#### September 11 attacks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: September 11 attacks")]

Main article: [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks")

[![The twin towers are seen spewing black smoke and flames, particularly from the left of the two](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/UA_Flight_175_hits_WTC_south_tower_9-11_edit.jpeg/250px-UA_Flight_175_hits_WTC_south_tower_9-11_edit.jpeg)](/wiki/File:UA_Flight_175_hits_WTC_south_tower_9-11_edit.jpeg)

[United Airlines Flight 175](/wiki/United_Airlines_Flight_175 "United Airlines Flight 175") hitting the [South Tower](/wiki/2_World_Trade_Center#Original_building_(1973–2001) "2 World Trade Center") during the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks")

[![Lower Manhattan's Avenue C is seen flooded.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Hurricane_Sandy_Flooding_Avenue_C_2012.JPG/250px-Hurricane_Sandy_Flooding_Avenue_C_2012.JPG)](/wiki/File:Hurricane_Sandy_Flooding_Avenue_C_2012.JPG)

Flooding on [Avenue C](/wiki/Avenue_C_(Manhattan) "Avenue C (Manhattan)") in [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan") caused by [Hurricane Sandy](/wiki/Hurricane_Sandy "Hurricane Sandy") in October 2012

On September 11, 2001, two of four hijacked planes were flown into the Twin Towers of the original [World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001) "World Trade Center (1973–2001)") in [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan"), and [the towers collapsed](/wiki/Collapse_of_the_World_Trade_Center "Collapse of the World Trade Center"). [7 World Trade Center](/wiki/7_World_Trade_Center_(1987%E2%80%932001) "7 World Trade Center (1987–2001)") also collapsed due to damage from fires. The other buildings of the World Trade Center complex were damaged beyond repair and demolished soon thereafter. The collapse of the Twin Towers caused extensive damage and resulted in the deaths of 2,753 victims, including 147 aboard the two planes. Since September 11, most of Lower Manhattan has been restored. In the years since, over 7,000 rescue workers and residents of the area have developed several life-threatening illnesses, and some have died.[[86]](#cite_note-89)[[87]](#cite_note-90)

A memorial at the site, the [National September 11 Memorial & Museum](/wiki/National_September_11_Memorial_%26_Museum "National September 11 Memorial & Museum"), was opened to the public on September 11, 2011. A permanent museum later opened at the site on March 21, 2014. Upon its completion in 2014, the new [One World Trade Center](/wiki/One_World_Trade_Center "One World Trade Center") became the [tallest skyscraper](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_New_York_City "List of tallest buildings in New York City") in the Western Hemisphere, at 1,776 feet (541 m), meant to symbolize the year [America gained its independence](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), 1776.[[88]](#cite_note-91) From 2006 to 2023, [3 World Trade Center](/wiki/3_World_Trade_Center "3 World Trade Center"), [4 World Trade Center](/wiki/4_World_Trade_Center "4 World Trade Center"), 7 World Trade Center, the [World Trade Center Transportation Hub](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_Transportation_Hub "World Trade Center Transportation Hub"), [Liberty Park](/wiki/Liberty_Park "Liberty Park"), [Fiterman Hall](/wiki/Borough_of_Manhattan_Community_College#Fiterman_Hall_and_the_September_11_attacks "Borough of Manhattan Community College"), [St. Nicholas Greek Orthodox Church](/wiki/St._Nicholas_Greek_Orthodox_Church "St. Nicholas Greek Orthodox Church"), and the [Ronald O. Perelman Performing Arts Center](/wiki/Performing_Arts_Center_(Manhattan) "Performing Arts Center (Manhattan)") were also constructed on the [World Trade Center site](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_site "World Trade Center site").

#### Hurricane Sandy (2012)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Hurricane Sandy (2012)")]

Main article: [Effects of Hurricane Sandy in New York](/wiki/Effects_of_Hurricane_Sandy_in_New_York "Effects of Hurricane Sandy in New York")

On October 29 and 30, 2012, [Hurricane Sandy](/wiki/Hurricane_Sandy "Hurricane Sandy") caused extensive destruction of the state's shorelines, ravaging portions of New York City, [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island"), and southern Westchester with record-high [storm surge](/wiki/Storm_surge "Storm surge"), with severe flooding and high winds causing [power outages](/wiki/Power_outage "Power outage") for hundreds of thousands of New Yorkers, and leading to [gasoline](/wiki/Gasoline "Gasoline") shortages and disruption of [mass transit](/wiki/Mass_transit "Mass transit") systems. The storm and its profound effects have prompted the discussion of constructing [seawalls](/wiki/Seawall "Seawall") and other [coastal barriers](/wiki/Coastal_management "Coastal management") around the shorelines of New York City and Long Island to minimize the risk from another such future event. Such risk is considered highly probable due to [global warming](/wiki/Global_warming "Global warming") and [rising sea levels](/wiki/Sea_level_rise "Sea level rise").[[89]](#cite_note-92)[[90]](#cite_note-93)

#### COVID-19 pandemic (2020–2023)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: COVID-19 pandemic (2020–2023)")]

Main articles: [COVID-19 pandemic in New York (state)](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_New_York_(state) "COVID-19 pandemic in New York (state)") and [Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on religion](/wiki/Impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic_on_religion "Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on religion")

On March 1, 2020, New York had its first confirmed case of [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_States "COVID-19 pandemic in the United States") after [Washington (state)](/wiki/Washington_(state) "Washington (state)"), two months prior.[[91]](#cite_note-94)

From May 19–20, Western New York and the [Capital Region](/wiki/Capital_District,_New_York "Capital District, New York") entered Phase 1 of reopening.[[92]](#cite_note-95)[[93]](#cite_note-96) On May 26, the Hudson Valley began Phase 1,[[94]](#cite_note-97) and New York City partially reopened on June 8.[[95]](#cite_note-98)

During July 2020, a federal judge ruled Governor [Andrew Cuomo](/wiki/Andrew_Cuomo "Andrew Cuomo") and Mayor [Bill de Blasio](/wiki/Bill_de_Blasio "Bill de Blasio") exceeded authority by limiting religious gatherings to 25% when others operated at 50% capacity.[[96]](#cite_note-99)[[97]](#cite_note-100)[[98]](#cite_note-101) On Thanksgiving Eve, the U.S. Supreme Court blocked additional religious restrictions imposed by Cuomo for areas with high infection rates.[[99]](#cite_note-102)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Geography")]

Main article: [Geography of New York (state)](/wiki/Geography_of_New_York_(state) "Geography of New York (state)")

[![A topographic map of the state of New York, with urban and geographic features marked](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/New_York_state_geographic_map-en.svg/250px-New_York_state_geographic_map-en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:New_York_state_geographic_map-en.svg)

New York is bordered by five U.S. states ([Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut"), [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts"), [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"), [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"), and [Vermont](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont")), two [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes") ([Lake Erie](/wiki/Lake_Erie "Lake Erie") and [Lake Ontario](/wiki/Lake_Ontario "Lake Ontario")), and two Canadian provinces ([Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario") and [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec")).

The state of New York covers a total area of 54,555 square miles (141,297 km2) and ranks as the [27th-largest state by size](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_area "List of U.S. states and territories by area").[[7]](#cite_note-area-7) The highest elevation in New York is [Mount Marcy](/wiki/Mount_Marcy "Mount Marcy") in the [Adirondack High Peaks](/wiki/Adirondack_High_Peaks "Adirondack High Peaks") in [Northern New York](/wiki/North_Country_(New_York) "North Country (New York)"), at 5,344 feet (1,629 meters) [above sea level](/wiki/Above_sea_level "Above sea level"); while the state's lowest point is at sea level, on the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") in [Downstate New York](/wiki/Downstate_New_York "Downstate New York").[[100]](#cite_note-usgs-103)

In contrast with New York City's urban landscape, the vast majority of the state's geographic area is dominated by [meadows](/wiki/Meadow "Meadow"), [forests](/wiki/Forest "Forest"), rivers, farms, mountains, and lakes. Most of the southern part of the state rests on the [Allegheny Plateau](/wiki/Allegheny_Plateau "Allegheny Plateau"), which extends from the [southeastern United States](/wiki/Southeastern_United_States "Southeastern United States") to the [Catskill Mountains](/wiki/Catskill_Mountains "Catskill Mountains"); the section in the State of New York is known as the [Southern Tier](/wiki/Southern_Tier "Southern Tier"). The rugged [Adirondack Mountains](/wiki/Adirondack_Mountains "Adirondack Mountains"), with vast tracts of wilderness, lie west of the [Lake Champlain Valley](/wiki/Lake_Champlain_Valley "Lake Champlain Valley"). [Lake George](/wiki/Lake_George_(New_York) "Lake George (New York)"), known as the *Queen of the Lakes* in the United States, drains through the [La Chute River](/wiki/La_Chute_River "La Chute River") into [Lake Champlain](/wiki/Lake_Champlain "Lake Champlain") at the eastern edge of the Adirondacks. The [Great Appalachian Valley](/wiki/Great_Appalachian_Valley "Great Appalachian Valley") dominates eastern New York and contains Lake Champlain Valley as its northern half and the [Hudson Valley](/wiki/Hudson_Valley "Hudson Valley") as its southern half within the state. The [Tug Hill](/wiki/Tug_Hill "Tug Hill") region arises as a [cuesta](/wiki/Cuesta "Cuesta") east of [Lake Ontario](/wiki/Lake_Ontario "Lake Ontario").[[101]](#cite_note-NYS_THComm_Region-104) The state of New York contains a part of the [Marcellus shale](/wiki/Marcellus_shale "Marcellus shale"), which extends into Ohio and Pennsylvania.[[102]](#cite_note-frack-105)

*Upstate* and *Downstate* are often used informally to distinguish New York City or its greater metropolitan area from the rest of the State of New York. The placement of a boundary between the two is a matter of great contention.[[103]](#cite_note-106) Unofficial and loosely defined regions of Upstate New York include from the Southern Tier, which includes many of the counties along the border with Pennsylvania,[[104]](#cite_note-107) to the [North Country](/wiki/North_Country_(New_York) "North Country (New York)") region, above or sometimes including parts of the Adirondack region.[[105]](#cite_note-108)

### Water

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Water")]

#### Borders

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Borders")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/New_York_City_and_the_Long_Island.jpg/250px-New_York_City_and_the_Long_Island.jpg)](/wiki/File:New_York_City_and_the_Long_Island.jpg)

Enveloped by the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") and [Long Island Sound](/wiki/Long_Island_Sound "Long Island Sound"), [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") and [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island") have a combined population of 11 million residents, representing over 56 percent of the state's population.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/K%C3%B6ppen_Climate_Types_New_York.png/250px-K%C3%B6ppen_Climate_Types_New_York.png)](/wiki/File:K%C3%B6ppen_Climate_Types_New_York.png)

The [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") of New York

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/LakeEffectSnowBuffalo101206.gif/250px-LakeEffectSnowBuffalo101206.gif)](/wiki/File:LakeEffectSnowBuffalo101206.gif)

[Lake-effect snow](/wiki/Lake-effect_snow "Lake-effect snow") is a major contributor to heavy snowfall totals in [Western New York](/wiki/Western_New_York "Western New York"), including the [Tug Hill](/wiki/Tug_Hill "Tug Hill") region.

Among the total area of New York state, 13.6% consists of water.[[106]](#cite_note-109) Much of New York's boundaries are in water, as is true for New York City: four of its [five boroughs](/wiki/Borough_(New_York_City) "Borough (New York City)") are situated on three islands at the mouth of the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River"): [Manhattan Island](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan"); [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island"); and [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island"), which contains [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn") and [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens") at its western end. The state's borders include a water boundary in (clockwise from the west) two [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes") ([Lake Erie](/wiki/Lake_Erie "Lake Erie") and [Lake Ontario](/wiki/Lake_Ontario "Lake Ontario"), which are connected by the [Niagara River](/wiki/Niagara_River "Niagara River")); the provinces of [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario") and [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec") in Canada, with New York and Ontario sharing the [Thousand Islands](/wiki/Thousand_Islands "Thousand Islands") [archipelago](/wiki/Archipelago "Archipelago") within the Saint Lawrence River, while most of its border with Quebec is on land; it shares [Lake Champlain](/wiki/Lake_Champlain "Lake Champlain") with the [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") state of [Vermont](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont"); the New England state of [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts") has mostly a land border; New York extends into [Long Island Sound](/wiki/Long_Island_Sound "Long Island Sound") and the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean"), sharing a water border with [Rhode Island](/wiki/Rhode_Island "Rhode Island"), while [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut") has land and sea borders. Except for areas near the [New York Harbor](/wiki/New_York_Harbor "New York Harbor") and the Upper [Delaware River](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River"), New York has a mostly land border with two [Mid-Atlantic states](/wiki/Mid-Atlantic_states "Mid-Atlantic states"), [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey") and [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"). New York is the only state that borders both the Great Lakes and the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean").

#### Drainage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Drainage")]

The [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River") begins near [Lake Tear of the Clouds](/wiki/Lake_Tear_of_the_Clouds "Lake Tear of the Clouds") and flows south through the eastern part of the state, without draining Lakes [George](/wiki/Lake_George_(lake),_New_York "Lake George (lake), New York") or [Champlain](/wiki/Lake_Champlain "Lake Champlain"). Lake George empties at its north end into Lake Champlain, whose northern end extends into Canada, where it drains into the [Richelieu River](/wiki/Richelieu_River "Richelieu River") and then ultimately the [Saint Lawrence River](/wiki/Saint_Lawrence_River "Saint Lawrence River"). The western section of the state is drained by the [Allegheny River](/wiki/Allegheny_River "Allegheny River") and rivers of the [Susquehanna](/wiki/Susquehanna_River "Susquehanna River") and [Delaware River](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River") systems. [Niagara Falls](/wiki/Niagara_Falls "Niagara Falls") is shared between New York and Ontario as it flows on the Niagara River from Lake Erie to Lake Ontario. The Delaware River Basin Compact, signed in 1961 by New York, New Jersey, [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"), [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware "Delaware"), and the federal government, regulates the utilization of water of the Delaware system.[[107]](#cite_note-110)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Climate")]

Main article: [Climate of New York (state)](/wiki/Climate_of_New_York_(state) "Climate of New York (state)")

Under the [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"), most of New York has a [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate"), though New York City and Long Island have a [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate").[[108]](#cite_note-NYClimate-111) Weather in New York is heavily influenced by two continental air masses: a warm, humid one from the southwest and a cold, dry one from the northwest. Downstate New York (comprising New York City, Long Island, and lower portions of the Hudson Valley) have rather hot summers with some periods of high humidity and cold, damp winters which are relatively mild compared to temperatures in Upstate New York, due to the downstate region's lower elevation, proximity to the Atlantic Ocean, and relatively lower [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude").

[Upstate New York](/wiki/Upstate_New_York "Upstate New York") experiences warm summers, marred by only occasional, brief intervals of sultry conditions, with long and cold winters. Western New York, particularly the Tug Hill region, receives heavy [lake-effect snows](/wiki/Lake-effect_snow "Lake-effect snow"), especially during the earlier portions of winter, before the surface of Lake Ontario itself is covered by ice. The summer climate is cool in the Adirondacks, Catskills, and at higher elevations of the Southern Tier. Buffalo and its metropolitan area are described as climate change havens for their weather pattern in Western New York.[[109]](#cite_note-112)[[110]](#cite_note-113)[[111]](#cite_note-114)[[112]](#cite_note-115)

Summer daytime temperatures range from the high 70s to low 80s °F (25 to 28 °C), over most of the state. In the majority of winter seasons, a temperature of −13 °F (−25 °C) or lower can be expected in the northern highlands (Northern Plateau) and 5 °F (−15 °C) or colder in the southwestern and east-central highlands of the Southern Tier. New York had a record-high temperature of 108 °F (42.2 °C) on July 22, 1926, in the [Albany](/wiki/Albany,_New_York "Albany, New York") area.[[113]](#cite_note-noaa-2020-116) Its record-lowest temperature during the winter was −52 °F (−46.7 °C) in 1979.[[113]](#cite_note-noaa-2020-116) [Governors Island, Manhattan](/wiki/Governors_Island,_Manhattan "Governors Island, Manhattan"), in [New York Harbor](/wiki/New_York_Harbor "New York Harbor"), is planned to host a US$1 billion research and education center poised to make New York the global leader in addressing the [climate crisis](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change").[[114]](#cite_note-NYCGlobalClimateLeader-117)

### Flora and fauna

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Flora and fauna")]

Due to New York's relatively large land area and unique geography compared to other eastern states, there are several distinct [ecoregions](/wiki/Ecoregions "Ecoregions") present in the state, many of them reduced heavily due to urbanization and other human activities: [Southern Great Lakes forests](/wiki/Southern_Great_Lakes_forests "Southern Great Lakes forests") in [Western New York](/wiki/Western_New_York "Western New York"), [New England–Acadian forests](/wiki/New_England%E2%80%93Acadian_forests "New England–Acadian forests") on the [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") border, [Northeastern coastal forests](/wiki/Northeastern_coastal_forests "Northeastern coastal forests") in the lower [Hudson Valley](/wiki/Hudson_Valley "Hudson Valley") and western [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island"), [Atlantic coastal pine barrens](/wiki/Atlantic_coastal_pine_barrens "Atlantic coastal pine barrens") in southern Long Island, [Northeastern interior dry–mesic oak forest](/wiki/Northeastern_interior_dry%E2%80%93mesic_oak_forest "Northeastern interior dry–mesic oak forest") in the eastern [Southern Tier](/wiki/Southern_Tier "Southern Tier") and upper Hudson Valley, [Appalachian–Blue Ridge forests](/wiki/Appalachian%E2%80%93Blue_Ridge_forests "Appalachian–Blue Ridge forests") in the Hudson Valley), [Central Appalachian dry oak–pine forest](/wiki/Central_Appalachian_dry_oak%E2%80%93pine_forest "Central Appalachian dry oak–pine forest") around the Hudson Valley, [Eastern Great Lakes and Hudson Lowlands](/wiki/Eastern_Great_Lakes_and_Hudson_Lowlands_(ecoregion) "Eastern Great Lakes and Hudson Lowlands (ecoregion)"), [Eastern forest–boreal transition](/wiki/Eastern_forest%E2%80%93boreal_transition "Eastern forest–boreal transition") in the Adirondacks, [Eastern Great Lakes lowland forests](/wiki/Eastern_Great_Lakes_lowland_forests "Eastern Great Lakes lowland forests") around the Adirondacks, and [Allegheny Highlands forests](/wiki/Allegheny_Highlands_forests "Allegheny Highlands forests"), most of which are in the western Southern Tier.

Some species that can be found in this state are [American ginseng](/wiki/American_ginseng "American ginseng"), [starry stonewort](/wiki/Nitellopsis_obtusa "Nitellopsis obtusa"), [waterthyme](/wiki/Hydrilla "Hydrilla"), water chestnut, [eastern poison ivy](/wiki/Toxicodendron_radicans "Toxicodendron radicans"), [poison sumac](/wiki/Toxicodendron_vernix "Toxicodendron vernix"), [giant hogweed](/wiki/Heracleum_mantegazzianum "Heracleum mantegazzianum"), [cow parsnip](/wiki/Heracleum_maximum "Heracleum maximum") and [common nettle](/wiki/Urtica_dioica "Urtica dioica").[[115]](#cite_note-118) There are more than 70 mammal species, more than 20 bird species, some species of amphibians, and several reptile species.

Species of mammals that are found in New York are the [white-footed mouse](/wiki/White-footed_mouse "White-footed mouse"), [North American least shrew](/wiki/North_American_least_shrew "North American least shrew"), [little brown bat](/wiki/Little_brown_bat "Little brown bat"), [muskrat](/wiki/Muskrat "Muskrat"), [eastern gray squirrel](/wiki/Eastern_gray_squirrel "Eastern gray squirrel"), [eastern cottontail](/wiki/Eastern_cottontail "Eastern cottontail"), [American ermine](/wiki/American_ermine "American ermine"), [groundhog](/wiki/Groundhog "Groundhog"), [striped skunk](/wiki/Striped_skunk "Striped skunk"), [fisher](/wiki/Fisher_(animal) "Fisher (animal)"), [North American river otter](/wiki/North_American_river_otter "North American river otter"), [raccoon](/wiki/Raccoon "Raccoon"), [bobcat](/wiki/Bobcat "Bobcat"), [eastern coyote](/wiki/Eastern_coyote "Eastern coyote"), [red fox](/wiki/Red_fox "Red fox"), [gray fox](/wiki/Gray_fox "Gray fox") [white-tailed deer](/wiki/White-tailed_deer "White-tailed deer"), [moose](/wiki/Moose "Moose"), and [American black bear](/wiki/American_black_bear "American black bear"); [extirpated](/wiki/Extirpated "Extirpated") mammals include [Canada lynx](/wiki/Canada_lynx "Canada lynx"), [American bison](/wiki/American_bison "American bison"), [wolverine](/wiki/Wolverine "Wolverine"), [Allegheny woodrat](/wiki/Allegheny_woodrat "Allegheny woodrat"), [caribou](/wiki/Caribou "Caribou"), [eastern elk](/wiki/Eastern_elk "Eastern elk"), [eastern cougar](/wiki/Eastern_cougar "Eastern cougar"), and [eastern wolf](/wiki/Eastern_wolf "Eastern wolf").[[116]](#cite_note-119) Some species of birds in New York are the [ring-necked pheasant](/wiki/Common_pheasant "Common pheasant"), [northern bobwhite](/wiki/Northern_bobwhite "Northern bobwhite"), [ruffed grouse](/wiki/Ruffed_grouse "Ruffed grouse"), [spruce grouse](/wiki/Spruce_grouse "Spruce grouse"), [Canada jay](/wiki/Canada_jay "Canada jay"), [wild turkey](/wiki/Wild_turkey "Wild turkey"), [blue jay](/wiki/Blue_jay "Blue jay"), [eastern bluebird](/wiki/Eastern_bluebird "Eastern bluebird") (the [state bird](/wiki/List_of_U.S._state_birds "List of U.S. state birds")), [American robin](/wiki/American_robin "American robin"), and [black-capped chickadee](/wiki/Black-capped_chickadee "Black-capped chickadee").

Birds of prey that are present in the state are [great horned owls](/wiki/Great_horned_owl "Great horned owl"), [bald eagles](/wiki/Bald_eagle "Bald eagle"), [red-tailed hawks](/wiki/Red-tailed_hawk "Red-tailed hawk"), [American kestrels](/wiki/American_kestrel "American kestrel"), and [northern harriers](/wiki/Northern_harrier "Northern harrier"). Waterfowl like [mallards](/wiki/Mallard "Mallard"), [wood ducks](/wiki/Wood_duck "Wood duck"), [canvasbacks](/wiki/Canvasback "Canvasback"), [American black ducks](/wiki/American_black_duck "American black duck"), [trumpeter swans](/wiki/Trumpeter_swan "Trumpeter swan"), [Canada geese](/wiki/Canada_goose "Canada goose"), and [blue-winged teals](/wiki/Blue-winged_teal "Blue-winged teal") can be found in the region. Maritime or shore birds of New York are [great blue heron](/wiki/Great_blue_heron "Great blue heron"), [killdeers](/wiki/Killdeer "Killdeer"), [northern cardinals](/wiki/Northern_cardinal "Northern cardinal"), [American herring gulls](/wiki/American_herring_gull "American herring gull"), and [common terns](/wiki/Common_tern "Common tern").[[117]](#cite_note-120) Reptile and amphibian species in land areas of New York include [queen snakes](/wiki/Queen_snake "Queen snake"), [hellbenders](/wiki/Hellbender "Hellbender"), [diamondback terrapins](/wiki/Diamondback_terrapin "Diamondback terrapin"), [timber rattlesnakes](/wiki/Timber_rattlesnake "Timber rattlesnake"), [eastern fence lizards](/wiki/Eastern_fence_lizard "Eastern fence lizard"), [spotted turtles](/wiki/Spotted_turtle "Spotted turtle"), and [Blanding's turtles](/wiki/Blanding%27s_turtle "Blanding's turtle"). Sea turtles that can be found in the state are the [green sea turtle](/wiki/Green_sea_turtle "Green sea turtle"), [loggerhead sea turtle](/wiki/Loggerhead_sea_turtle "Loggerhead sea turtle"), [leatherback sea turtle](/wiki/Leatherback_sea_turtle "Leatherback sea turtle") and [Kemp's ridley sea turtle](/wiki/Kemp%27s_ridley_sea_turtle "Kemp's ridley sea turtle").[[118]](#cite_note-121) [New York Harbor](/wiki/New_York_Harbor "New York Harbor") and the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River") constitute an [estuary](/wiki/Estuary "Estuary"), making the state of New York home to a [rich array of marine life](/wiki/Marine_life_of_New_York%E2%80%93New_Jersey_Harbor_Estuary "Marine life of New York–New Jersey Harbor Estuary") including shellfish—such as [oysters](/wiki/Oysters "Oysters") and [clams](/wiki/Clams "Clams")—as well as fish, microorganisms, and sea-birds.
