### Regions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Regions")]

Main article: [List of regions of the United States § New York](/wiki/List_of_regions_of_the_United_States#New_York "List of regions of the United States")

Due to its long history, New York has several overlapping and often conflicting definitions of regions within the state. The regions are also not fully definable due to the colloquial use of regional labels. The [New York State Department of Economic Development](/wiki/Empire_State_Development_Corporation "Empire State Development Corporation") provides two distinct definitions of these regions. It divides the state's counties into ten economic regions,[[119]](#cite_note-122) which approximately correspond to terminology used by residents:

1. [Western New York](/wiki/Western_New_York "Western New York")
2. [Finger Lakes](/wiki/Finger_Lakes "Finger Lakes")
3. [Southern Tier](/wiki/Southern_Tier "Southern Tier")
4. [Central New York](/wiki/Central_New_York "Central New York")
5. [North Country](/wiki/North_Country_(New_York) "North Country (New York)")
6. [Mohawk Valley](/wiki/Mohawk_Valley "Mohawk Valley")
7. [Capital District](/wiki/Capital_District,_New_York "Capital District, New York")
8. [Hudson Valley](/wiki/Hudson_Valley "Hudson Valley")
9. [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City")
10. [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island")

The department also groups the counties into eleven regions for tourism purposes.[[120]](#cite_note-123)[[121]](#cite_note-124)

1. [Chautauqua–Allegheny](/w/index.php?title=Chautauqua%E2%80%93Allegheny&action=edit&redlink=1 "Chautauqua–Allegheny (page does not exist)")
2. [Niagara Frontier](/wiki/Niagara_Frontier "Niagara Frontier")
3. [Finger Lakes](/wiki/Finger_Lakes "Finger Lakes")
4. [Thousand Islands](/wiki/Thousand_Islands "Thousand Islands")
5. [Central-Leatherstocking Region](/wiki/Central_New_York_Region "Central New York Region")
6. [Adirondack Mountains](/wiki/Adirondack_Mountains "Adirondack Mountains")
7. [Capital District](/wiki/Capital_District,_New_York "Capital District, New York")
8. [Catskill Mountains](/wiki/Catskill_Mountains "Catskill Mountains")
9. [Hudson Valley](/wiki/Hudson_Valley "Hudson Valley")
10. [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City")
11. [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island")

### State parks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: State parks")]

See also: [List of New York state parks](/wiki/List_of_New_York_state_parks "List of New York state parks") and [New York State Office of Parks, Recreation and Historic Preservation](/wiki/New_York_State_Office_of_Parks,_Recreation_and_Historic_Preservation "New York State Office of Parks, Recreation and Historic Preservation")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Adirondack_and_Catskill_Parks_Locator.svg/250px-Adirondack_and_Catskill_Parks_Locator.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Adirondack_and_Catskill_Parks_Locator.svg)

Two major New York state parks (in green), [Adirondack Park](/wiki/Adirondack_Park "Adirondack Park") (north) and [Catskill Park](/wiki/Catskill_Park "Catskill Park") (south)

New York has many state parks and two major forest preserves. [Niagara Falls State Park](/wiki/Niagara_Falls_State_Park "Niagara Falls State Park"), established in 1885, is the oldest state park in the United States and the first to be created via [eminent domain](/wiki/Eminent_domain "Eminent domain").[[122]](#cite_note-NYSA_EnvHist-125)[[123]](#cite_note-NFSP_NPS_study-126) In 1892, [Adirondack Park](/wiki/Adirondack_Park "Adirondack Park"), roughly the size of the state of [Vermont](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont") and the largest state park in the United States,[[124]](#cite_note-lakeplacid.com-127) was established and given state constitutional protection to remain "forever wild" in 1894. The park is larger than [Yellowstone](/wiki/Yellowstone_National_Park "Yellowstone National Park"), [Everglades](/wiki/Everglades_National_Park "Everglades National Park"), [Glacier](/wiki/Glacier_National_Park_(U.S.) "Glacier National Park (U.S.)"), and [Grand Canyon](/wiki/Grand_Canyon_National_Park "Grand Canyon National Park") national parks combined.[[124]](#cite_note-lakeplacid.com-127)[[125]](#cite_note-128) The [Catskill Park](/wiki/Catskill_Park "Catskill Park") was protected in legislation passed in 1885,[[126]](#cite_note-CatskillPark1-129) which declared that its land was to be conserved and never put up for sale or lease. Consisting of 700,000 acres (2,800 km2) of land,[[126]](#cite_note-CatskillPark1-129) the park is a habitat for deer, [minks](/wiki/Mink "Mink"), and fishers. There are some 400 [black bears](/wiki/American_black_bear "American black bear") living in the region.[[127]](#cite_note-130) The state operates numerous campgrounds, and there are over 300 miles (480 km) of multi-use trails in the Park.

The 1797 [Montauk Lighthouse](/wiki/Montauk_Point_Light "Montauk Point Light"), commissioned under [President](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington"), is a major tourist attraction in [Montauk Point State Park](/wiki/Montauk_Point_State_Park "Montauk Point State Park") at the easternmost tip of [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island"). [Hither Hills State Park](/wiki/Hither_Hills_State_Park "Hither Hills State Park"), also on Long Island's [South Fork](/wiki/South_Fork_(Long_Island) "South Fork (Long Island)"), offers camping and is a popular destination with surfcasting sport fishermen.

### National parks, monuments, and historic landmarks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: National parks, monuments, and historic landmarks")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Statue_of_Liberty%2C_NY.jpg/250px-Statue_of_Liberty%2C_NY.jpg)](/wiki/File:Statue_of_Liberty,_NY.jpg)

The [Statue of Liberty](/wiki/Statue_of_Liberty "Statue of Liberty") in [New York Harbor](/wiki/New_York_Harbor "New York Harbor"), a global symbol of the United States and its ideals[[128]](#cite_note-131)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/African_Burial_Ground-aerial_view-NYC.jpg/250px-African_Burial_Ground-aerial_view-NYC.jpg)](/wiki/File:African_Burial_Ground-aerial_view-NYC.jpg)

The [African Burial Ground National Monument](/wiki/African_Burial_Ground_National_Monument "African Burial Ground National Monument") in [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan")

The State of New York is well represented in the [National Park System](/wiki/National_Park_Service "National Park Service") with 22 [national parks](/wiki/National_park "National park"), which received 16,349,381 visitors in 2011. In addition, there are a [national marine sanctuary](/wiki/National_Marine_Sanctuary "National Marine Sanctuary"), four [national heritage areas](/wiki/National_Heritage_Area "National Heritage Area"), 27 [national natural landmarks](/wiki/National_Natural_Landmark "National Natural Landmark"), 262 [national historic landmarks](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark"), and 5,379 listings on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places"). Some major areas, landmarks, and monuments are listed below.

* The [Statue of Liberty National Monument](/wiki/Statue_of_Liberty_National_Monument "Statue of Liberty National Monument") includes [Ellis Island](/wiki/Ellis_Island "Ellis Island") and the [Statue of Liberty](/wiki/Statue_of_Liberty "Statue of Liberty"). The statue, designed by [Frédéric Bartholdi](/wiki/Fr%C3%A9d%C3%A9ric_Auguste_Bartholdi "Frédéric Auguste Bartholdi") and formally named *Liberty Enlightening the World*, was a gift from [France](/wiki/France "France") to the United States to mark the Centennial of the [American Declaration of Independence](/wiki/Declaration_of_Independence_of_the_United_States "Declaration of Independence of the United States"); it was dedicated in New York Harbor on October 28, 1886. It has since become an icon of the United States and the concepts of democracy and freedom.
* The [African Burial Ground National Monument](/wiki/African_Burial_Ground_National_Monument "African Burial Ground National Monument") in [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan") is the only national monument dedicated to Americans of African ancestry. It preserves a site containing the remains of more than 400 [Africans](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_of_Africa "Ethnic groups of Africa") buried during the late 17th and 18th centuries in a portion of what was the largest colonial-era cemetery for people of African descent, both free and enslaved, with an estimated tens of thousands of remains interred. The site's excavation and study were called "the most important historic urban archeological project in the United States."[[129]](#cite_note-ABG-132)
* [Fire Island National Seashore](/wiki/Fire_Island_National_Seashore "Fire Island National Seashore") is a [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") [national seashore](/wiki/List_of_United_States_national_lakeshores_and_seashores "List of United States national lakeshores and seashores") that protects a 26-mile (42 km) section of [Fire Island](/wiki/Fire_Island "Fire Island"), an approximately 30-mile (48 km) long [barrier island](/wiki/Barrier_island "Barrier island") separated from the mainland of [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island") by the [Great South Bay](/wiki/Great_South_Bay "Great South Bay"). The island is part of [Suffolk County](/wiki/Suffolk_County,_New_York "Suffolk County, New York").[[130]](#cite_note-133)
* [Gateway National Recreation Area](/wiki/Gateway_National_Recreation_Area "Gateway National Recreation Area") is more than 26,000 acres (10,522 ha) of water, [salt marsh](/wiki/Salt_marsh "Salt marsh"), [wetlands](/wiki/Wetlands "Wetlands"), islands, and shoreline at the entrance to New York Harbor,[[131]](#cite_note-134) the majority of which lies within New York. Including areas on Long Island and in New Jersey, it covers more area than that of two Manhattan islands.
* [General Grant National Memorial](/wiki/General_Grant_National_Memorial "General Grant National Memorial") is the final resting place of President [Ulysses S. Grant](/wiki/Ulysses_S._Grant "Ulysses S. Grant") and is the largest [mausoleum](/wiki/Mausoleum "Mausoleum") in North America.
* [Hamilton Grange National Memorial](/wiki/Hamilton_Grange_National_Memorial "Hamilton Grange National Memorial") preserves the home of [Alexander Hamilton](/wiki/Alexander_Hamilton "Alexander Hamilton"), Caribbean immigrant and [orphan](/wiki/Orphan "Orphan") who rose to be a United States founding father and associate of George Washington.
* The [Home of Franklin D. Roosevelt National Historic Site](/wiki/Home_of_Franklin_D._Roosevelt_National_Historic_Site "Home of Franklin D. Roosevelt National Historic Site"), established in 1945, preserves the Springwood estate in [Hyde Park, New York](/wiki/Hyde_Park,_New_York "Hyde Park, New York"). Springwood was the birthplace, lifelong home, and burial place of the 32nd [President of the United States](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States"), [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt").
* The [Lake Ontario National Marine Sanctuary](/wiki/Lake_Ontario_National_Marine_Sanctuary "Lake Ontario National Marine Sanctuary") was designated by the [National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration](/wiki/National_Oceanic_and_Atmospheric_Administration "National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration") in 2024; it covers 1,722 square miles (1,300 sq nmi; 4,460 km2) of southeastern [Lake Ontario](/wiki/Lake_Ontario "Lake Ontario") off the coasts of [Jefferson](/wiki/Jefferson_County,_New_York "Jefferson County, New York"), [Oswego](/wiki/Oswego_County,_New_York "Oswego County, New York"), [Cayuga](/wiki/Cayuga_County,_New_York "Cayuga County, New York"), and [Wayne](/wiki/Wayne_County,_New_York "Wayne County, New York") [counties](/wiki/County_(United_States) "County (United States)") and protects historic [shipwrecks](/wiki/Shipwreck "Shipwreck") and an area of cultural, historical, and spiritual importance to [Native Americans](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States"). It is managed jointly by NOAA's [Office of National Marine Sanctuaries](/wiki/Office_of_National_Marine_Sanctuaries "Office of National Marine Sanctuaries") and the State of New York.[[132]](#cite_note-135)[[133]](#cite_note-136)
* [Niagara Falls National Heritage Area](/wiki/Niagara_Falls_National_Heritage_Area "Niagara Falls National Heritage Area") was designated by the [U.S. Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") in 2008; it stretches from the western boundary of [Wheatfield, New York](/wiki/Wheatfield,_New_York "Wheatfield, New York") to the mouth of the [Niagara River](/wiki/Niagara_River "Niagara River") on Lake [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario"), including the communities of Niagara Falls, Youngstown, and Lewiston. It includes [Niagara Falls State Park](/wiki/Niagara_Falls_State_Park "Niagara Falls State Park") and [Colonial Niagara Historic District](/wiki/Fort_Niagara "Fort Niagara"). It is managed in collaboration with the state.
* [Saratoga National Historical Park](/wiki/Saratoga_National_Historical_Park "Saratoga National Historical Park") preserves the site of the [Battles of Saratoga](/wiki/Battles_of_Saratoga "Battles of Saratoga"), the first significant [American military](/wiki/United_States_Armed_Forces "United States Armed Forces") victory of the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"). In 1777, American forces defeated a major [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army"),[[68]](#cite_note-History-2020-71) which led [France](/wiki/France "France") to recognize the independence of the United States, and enter the war as a decisive military ally of the struggling Americans.
* [Stonewall National Monument](/wiki/Stonewall_National_Monument "Stonewall National Monument"), in the [Greenwich Village](/wiki/Greenwich_Village "Greenwich Village") neighborhood of Lower Manhattan, is the first U.S. national monument dedicated to [LGBTQ rights](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_the_United_States "LGBT rights in the United States"), designated on June 24, 2016. The monument comprises the area around the still privately operated [Stonewall Inn](/wiki/Stonewall_Inn "Stonewall Inn"), commonly recognized to be the cradle of the [gay liberation](/wiki/Gay_liberation "Gay liberation") movement as the site of the 1969 [Stonewall Riots](/wiki/Stonewall_Riots "Stonewall Riots"); the adjacent [Christopher Park](/wiki/Christopher_Street "Christopher Street"); and surrounding streets and sidewalks.[[134]](#cite_note-NYTRosenberg20160624-137)[[135]](#cite_note-NPSStonewallMonument-138)[[136]](#cite_note-LATHayasaki20070518-139)
* Manhattan's [Theodore Roosevelt Birthplace National Historic Site](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt_Birthplace_National_Historic_Site "Theodore Roosevelt Birthplace National Historic Site") is also the childhood home of President Theodore Roosevelt, the only president born in New York City until [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump").

### Administrative divisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Administrative divisions")]

Main article: [Administrative divisions of New York (state)](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_New_York_(state) "Administrative divisions of New York (state)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/New_York_municipalities.svg/250px-New_York_municipalities.svg.png)](/wiki/File:New_York_municipalities.svg)

[New York counties](/wiki/List_of_counties_in_New_York "List of counties in New York"), [municipalities](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_New_York "List of municipalities in New York"), and [towns](/wiki/List_of_towns_in_New_York "List of towns in New York").

As of 2022, New York is divided into 62 [counties](/wiki/County_(United_States) "County (United States)").[[137]](#cite_note-140) Aside from the five counties of New York City, each of these counties is subdivided into [towns](/wiki/Town#New_York "Town") and [cities](/wiki/City "City"), incorporated under state law. Towns can contain incorporated [villages](/wiki/Village "Village") or unincorporated [hamlets](/wiki/Hamlet_(place)#New_York "Hamlet (place)"). New York City is divided into five [boroughs](/wiki/Borough_(New_York_City) "Borough (New York City)"), each coterminous with a county. The major cities of the state developed along the key transportation and [trade routes](/wiki/Trade_route "Trade route") of the early 19th century, including the [Erie Canal](/wiki/Erie_Canal "Erie Canal") and railroads paralleling it. The [New York Thruway](/wiki/New_York_Thruway "New York Thruway") acts as a modern counterpart to commercial water routes.[[138]](#cite_note-141) [Downstate New York](/wiki/Downstate_New_York "Downstate New York") ([New York City](/wiki/Geography_of_New_York_City "Geography of New York City"), [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island"), and the southern portion of the [Hudson Valley](/wiki/Hudson_Valley "Hudson Valley")) can be considered to form the central core of the [Northeast megalopolis](/wiki/Northeast_megalopolis "Northeast megalopolis"), an urbanized region stretching from [New Hampshire](/wiki/New_Hampshire "New Hampshire") to [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia").

Further information: [List of towns in New York](/wiki/List_of_towns_in_New_York "List of towns in New York"), [List of villages in New York](/wiki/List_of_villages_in_New_York "List of villages in New York"), [List of census-designated places in New York](/wiki/List_of_census-designated_places_in_New_York "List of census-designated places in New York"), and [New York statistical areas](/wiki/New_York_statistical_areas "New York statistical areas")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/New_York_City%2C_night.jpg/250px-New_York_City%2C_night.jpg)](/wiki/File:New_York_City,_night.jpg)

New York City at night

New York contains 62 administrative divisions termed cities. The largest city in the state and the most populous city in the United States is New York City, which comprises five counties (each coextensive with a [borough](/wiki/Borough_(New_York_City) "Borough (New York City)")): [Bronx](/wiki/Bronx "Bronx"), New York County ([Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan")), [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens"), Kings County ([Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn")), and Richmond County ([Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island")). New York City is home to more than two-fifths of the state's population. [Albany](/wiki/Albany,_New_York "Albany, New York"), the state capital, is the sixth-largest city in the State of New York. The smallest city is [Sherrill, New York](/wiki/Sherrill,_New_York "Sherrill, New York"), in [Oneida County](/wiki/Oneida_County,_New_York "Oneida County, New York"). [Hempstead](/wiki/Hempstead,_New_York "Hempstead, New York") is the most populous [town](/wiki/Town_(New_York) "Town (New York)") in the state; if it were a city, it would be the second-largest in the State of New York, with more than 700,000 residents. New York contains 13 [metropolitan areas](/wiki/Metropolitan_areas "Metropolitan areas"), as defined by the U.S. Census Bureau.[[139]](#cite_note-142) Major metro areas include New York City, [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York"), [Rochester](/wiki/Rochester,_New_York "Rochester, New York"), the [Capital District](/wiki/Capital_District,_New_York "Capital District, New York") ([Albany](/wiki/Albany,_New_York "Albany, New York"), [Schenectady](/wiki/Schenectady,_New_York "Schenectady, New York"), and [Troy](/wiki/Troy,_New_York "Troy, New York")), [Poughkeepsie](/wiki/Poughkeepsie,_New_York "Poughkeepsie, New York"), [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_New_York "Syracuse, New York"), [Utica](/wiki/Utica,_New_York "Utica, New York"), and [Binghamton](/wiki/Binghamton,_New_York "Binghamton, New York").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/New_York_population_density_2020.png/330px-New_York_population_density_2020.png)](/wiki/File:New_York_population_density_2020.png)

Among New York state's population of 19.5 million, 11 million, or 56 percent, are in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") or [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island")

New York was the most populous state in the U.S. from the 1810s until 1962. As of 2024, it is the nation's fourth-most populous state behind [California](/wiki/California "California"), [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas"), and [Florida](/wiki/Florida "Florida"). Growth has been distributed unevenly. The [New York metropolitan area](/wiki/New_York_metropolitan_area "New York metropolitan area") and the [Capital District](/wiki/Capital_District_(New_York) "Capital District (New York)") (particularly [Saratoga County](/wiki/Saratoga_County,_New_York "Saratoga County, New York")) have been growing robustly overall, while the [Buffalo–Niagara Falls metropolitan area](/wiki/Buffalo%E2%80%93Niagara_Falls_metropolitan_area "Buffalo–Niagara Falls metropolitan area"), [Rochester](/wiki/Rochester,_New_York "Rochester, New York"), [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_New_York "Syracuse, New York"), and other population centers have been losing residents or have been stagnant for decades before resuming growth more recently.[[142]](#cite_note-2016NYCountyGrowthCensus-145) New York City gained 223,615 residents between April 2010 and July 2018, representing the greatest population increase of any U.S. city.[[142]](#cite_note-2016NYCountyGrowthCensus-145)

According to [immigration](/wiki/Immigration "Immigration") statistics, the state is a leading recipient of migrants from around the globe. In 2008 New York had the second-largest international immigrant population in the country among U.S. states, at 4.2 million; most reside in and around New York City, due to its size, high profile, vibrant economy, and [cosmopolitan](/wiki/Cosmopolitanism "Cosmopolitanism") culture. New York has a [sanctuary city](/wiki/Sanctuary_city "Sanctuary city") law.[[143]](#cite_note-146)

The [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau") tabulated in the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census") that the population of New York was 20,215,751 on April 1, 2020, a 4.3% increase since the [2010 census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census").[[10]](#cite_note-NewYorkQuickFacts-13)[[144]](#cite_note-147) Despite the abundance of open land in the state, New York's population is very urban, with 92% of residents living in an urban area,[[145]](#cite_note-148) predominantly in the New York City metropolitan area.

Two-thirds of the state's population resides in the [New York metropolitan area](/wiki/New_York_metropolitan_area "New York metropolitan area"). New York City, with approximately 44% of the state's population, is the most populous city in the United States,[[146]](#cite_note-149) with an estimated record high population of 8,622,698 in 2017,[[147]](#cite_note-NYCest2-150) incorporating more immigration into the city than emigration since the 2010 United States census.[[148]](#cite_note-inmigration-151) More than twice as many people live in New York City as in the second-most populous U.S. city, [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"),[[149]](#cite_note-152) and within a smaller area. [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island") alone accounted for a census-estimated 7,838,722 residents in 2015, representing nearly 40% of the State of New York's population.[[147]](#cite_note-NYCest2-150)[[150]](#cite_note-Kings_County,_New_York_QuickFacts-153)[[151]](#cite_note-Queens_County,_New_York_QuickFacts-154)[[152]](#cite_note-Nassau_County,_New_York_QuickFacts-155)[[153]](#cite_note-Suffolk_County,_New_York_QuickFacts-156) Of the total statewide population, 6.5% of New Yorkers were under five years of age, 24.7% under 18, and 12.9% were 65 or older.

According to [HUD](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Housing_and_Urban_Development "United States Department of Housing and Urban Development")'s 2022 [Annual Homeless Assessment Report](/wiki/Annual_Homeless_Assessment_Report_to_Congress "Annual Homeless Assessment Report to Congress"), there were an estimated 74,178 [homeless](/wiki/Homelessness "Homelessness") people in New York.[[154]](#cite_note-157)[[155]](#cite_note-158)

In 2017, the leading out-of-state birthplaces for New York residents were the [Dominican Republic](/wiki/Dominican_Republic "Dominican Republic"), [China](/wiki/China "China"), [India](/wiki/India "India"), [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico "Puerto Rico"), [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"), [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"), [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"), and Central American countries.[[156]](#cite_note-159)

According to the 2000 census, Italian, Irish, German, African American and English were the most common ancestries.[[158]](#cite_note-162)

The state's historically most populous racial group, non-Hispanic White people, declined as a proportion of the state population from 94.6% in 1940 to 58.3% in 2010.[[159]](#cite_note-PopUS-163)[[160]](#cite_note-census1-164) As of 2011[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit), 55.6% of New York's population younger than age 1 were minorities.[[161]](#cite_note-165) New York's robustly increasing [Jewish](/wiki/Jews "Jews") population, the largest outside of [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel"),[[162]](#cite_note-166) was the highest among states both by percentage and by absolute number in 2012.[[163]](#cite_note-167) It is driven by the high reproductive rate of [Orthodox Jewish](/wiki/Orthodox_Judaism "Orthodox Judaism") families,[[164]](#cite_note-168) particularly in [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn") and communities of the [Hudson Valley](/wiki/Hudson_Valley "Hudson Valley").

New York is home to the [second-largest Asian American](/wiki/Demographics_of_Asian_Americans "Demographics of Asian Americans") population and the [fourth-largest Black or African American](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_by_African-American_population "List of U.S. states by African-American population") population in the United States. New York's Black and African population increased by 2.0% between 2000 and 2010, to 3,073,800.[[165]](#cite_note-NYQuickLinks-169) In 2019, the Black and African American population increased to an estimated 3,424,002. The Black or African American population is in a state of flux, as New York is the largest recipient of immigrants from [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa"),[[166]](#cite_note-Chinese2013est-170) while established Blacks and African Americans are migrating out of New York to the [southern United States](/wiki/New_Great_Migration "New Great Migration").[[167]](#cite_note-171) The New York City neighborhood of [Harlem](/wiki/Harlem "Harlem") has historically been a major cultural capital for Blacks and African Americans of sub-Saharan descent, and [Bedford-Stuyvesant](/wiki/Bedford%E2%80%93Stuyvesant,_Brooklyn "Bedford–Stuyvesant, Brooklyn") in Brooklyn has the largest such population in the United States. Meanwhile, New York's Asian population increased by a notable 36% from 2000 to 2010, to 1,420,244;[[165]](#cite_note-NYQuickLinks-169) in 2019, its population grew to an estimated 1,579,494. [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens"), in New York City, is home to the state's largest Asian American population and is the most [ethnically diverse](/wiki/Ethnic_diversity "Ethnic diversity") county in the United States and the most ethnically diverse urban area in the world.[[168]](#cite_note-172)[[169]](#cite_note-173)

New York's growing Hispanic and Latino American population numbered 3,416,922 in 2010,[[170]](#cite_note-174) a 19% increase from the 2,867,583 enumerated in 2000.[[171]](#cite_note-175) In 2020, it numbered an estimated 3,811,000. Queens is home to the largest [Andean](/wiki/Andes "Andes") ([Colombian](/wiki/Colombian_American "Colombian American"), [Ecuadorian](/wiki/Ecuadorian_American "Ecuadorian American"), [Peruvian](/wiki/Peruvian_American "Peruvian American"), and [Bolivian](/wiki/Bolivian_American "Bolivian American")) populations in the United States. In addition, New York has the largest [Puerto Rican](/wiki/Puerto_Rican_people "Puerto Rican people"), [Dominican](/wiki/Dominican_American "Dominican American"), and [Jamaican American](/wiki/Jamaican_American "Jamaican American") populations in the continental United States. The [Chinese](/wiki/Overseas_Chinese "Overseas Chinese") population constitutes the fastest-growing nationality in the State of New York, which is the top destination for new Chinese immigrants, and large-scale [Chinese immigration](/wiki/Chinese_emigration "Chinese emigration") continues into the state.[[166]](#cite_note-Chinese2013est-170)[[172]](#cite_note-Chinese2012est-176)[[173]](#cite_note-Chinese2011est-177)[[174]](#cite_note-Chinese2010est-178)[[175]](#cite_note-179) Multiple [satellites](/wiki/Natural_satellite "Natural satellite") of the original [Manhattan Chinatown](/wiki/Chinatown,_Manhattan "Chinatown, Manhattan"), in [Brooklyn](/wiki/Chinatown,_Brooklyn "Chinatown, Brooklyn"), and around [Flushing, Queens](/wiki/Chinatown,_Flushing "Chinatown, Flushing"), are thriving as traditionally urban enclaves, while also expanding rapidly eastward into suburban [Nassau County](/wiki/Nassau_County,_New_York "Nassau County, New York"),[[176]](#cite_note-180) on [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island").[[177]](#cite_note-181) Long Island, including Queens and Nassau County, is also home to several [Little Indias](/wiki/Curry_Hill "Curry Hill") and a large [Koreatown](/wiki/Koreatown,_Long_Island "Koreatown, Long Island"), with large and growing attendant populations of [Indian Americans](/wiki/Indian_American "Indian American") and [Korean Americans](/wiki/Korean_American "Korean American"), respectively. Brooklyn has been a destination for [West Indian](/wiki/West_Indian "West Indian") immigrants of African descent, as well as Asian Indian immigrants. The annual New York City India Day Parade, held on or approximately every August 15 since 1981, is the world's largest [Indian Independence Day](/wiki/Indian_Independence_Day "Indian Independence Day") parade outside of India.[[178]](#cite_note-NYCLargestIndiaParade-182)

In the 2000 U.S. census, New York had the largest [Italian American](/wiki/Italian_American "Italian American") population, composing the largest self-identified ancestral group in [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island") and Long Island, followed by [Irish Americans](/wiki/Irish_American "Irish American"). Albany and the [Mohawk Valley](/wiki/Mohawk_Valley "Mohawk Valley") also have large communities of ethnic Italians and Irish Americans, reflecting 19th and early 20th-century immigration. According to the 2011–2015 [American Community Survey](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey"), New York also had the largest [Greek American](/wiki/Greek_Americans "Greek Americans") population, enumerating 148,637 individuals (0.7% of the state).[[179]](#cite_note-pop1-183) In [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York") and Western New York, [German Americans](/wiki/German_Americans "German Americans") comprise the largest ancestry. In the [North Country](/wiki/North_Country_(New_York) "North Country (New York)") of New York, [French Canadians](/wiki/French_Canadians "French Canadians") represent the leading ethnicity, given the area's proximity to [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"). Americans of [English](/wiki/English_American "English American") ancestry are present throughout all of upstate New York, reflecting early colonial and later immigrants. There is also a [Romani (Gypsy)](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people") community in New York.[[180]](#cite_note-184) [Scottish Americans](/wiki/Scottish_Americans "Scottish Americans") are also present in the state.[[181]](#cite_note-185) [Mexican Americans](/wiki/Mexican_Americans "Mexican Americans") are one of the largest Latino groups in New York.[[182]](#cite_note-186)

In 2019, the U.S. Census Bureau reported that 69.5% of New York's population aged 5 years and older only spoke [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") at home, while 30.6% spoke a language other than English. [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language") was the most spoken non-English language with 2,758,925 speakers. Other [Indo-European languages](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages") were spoken by 1,587,798 residents, and Asian and Pacific Island languages were spoken by 948,959 people.[[188]](#cite_note-192)

At the American Community Survey's 2017 estimates, nearly six million residents spoke a language other than English. Approximately 1,249,541 New York residents spoke Spanish, 386,290 [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"), 122,150 [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"), 63,615 [Haitian Creole](/wiki/Haitian_Creole "Haitian Creole"), 62,219 [Kannada](/wiki/Kannada_language "Kannada language"), 58,112
[Bengali](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language") and 60,405 [Korean](/wiki/Korean_language "Korean language"). While Kannada and Bengali are significantly growing Indian languages in the New York.[[189]](#cite_note-193)[[187]](#cite_note-MLA_Data-191) In 2018, 12,756,975 aged 5 years and older spoke English alone and 10,415,395 aged 18 and older only spoke English. Spanish-speaking households by majority were not limited to English-speaking.[[190]](#cite_note-194) An estimated 2.7 million households with residents aged 5 and older spoke Spanish. Chinese, Slavic, and [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") languages were the following largest household languages spoken in 2018.[[191]](#cite_note-195)

In 2010, 70.72% (12,788,233) of New York residents aged five and older reported speaking only English at home, while 14.44% (2,611,903) spoke Spanish, 2.61% (472,955) Chinese (which includes [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese") and [Mandarin](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese")), 1.20% (216,468) Russian, 1.18% (213,785) [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"), 0.79% (142,169) [French Creole](/wiki/French-based_creole_languages "French-based creole languages"), 0.75% (135,789) French, 0.67% (121,917) [Yiddish](/wiki/Yiddish_language "Yiddish language"), 0.63% (114,574) Korean, and [Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language") was spoken by 0.53% (95,413) of the population over the age of five. In total, 29.28% (5,295,016) of New York's population aged five and older reported speaking a language other than English.[[187]](#cite_note-MLA_Data-191)

In 2010, the most common [American English](/wiki/American_English "American English") [dialects](/wiki/Dialect "Dialect") spoken in New York, besides [General American English](/wiki/General_American_English "General American English"), were the [New York City area dialect](/wiki/New_York_dialect "New York dialect") (including [New York Latino English](/wiki/New_York_Latino_English "New York Latino English") and [North Jersey English](/wiki/New_Jersey_English_dialects#North_Jersey_English "New Jersey English dialects")), the [Western New England accent](/wiki/Western_New_England_dialect "Western New England dialect") around [Albany](/wiki/Albany,_New_York "Albany, New York"), and [Inland Northern American English](/wiki/Inland_Northern_American_English "Inland Northern American English") in Buffalo and western New York State. As many as 800 languages are spoken in New York City,[[192]](#cite_note-196)[[193]](#cite_note-800source2-197)[[194]](#cite_note-800source3-198) making it the most linguistically diverse city in the world.[[195]](#cite_note-199)

### Sexual orientation and gender identity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Sexual orientation and gender identity")]

Further information: [Greenwich Village Halloween Parade](/wiki/Greenwich_Village_Halloween_Parade "Greenwich Village Halloween Parade"), [LGBT culture in New York City](/wiki/LGBT_culture_in_New_York_City "LGBT culture in New York City"), [Transgender culture of New York City](/wiki/Transgender_culture_of_New_York_City "Transgender culture of New York City"), [LGBT rights in New York](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_New_York "LGBT rights in New York"), [List of largest LGBT events](/wiki/List_of_largest_LGBT_events "List of largest LGBT events"), [NYC Pride March](/wiki/NYC_Pride_March "NYC Pride March"), [List of LGBT people from New York City](/wiki/List_of_LGBT_people_from_New_York_City "List of LGBT people from New York City"), and [Same-sex marriage in New York](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_in_New_York "Same-sex marriage in New York")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Stonewall_Inn_5_pride_weekend_2016.jpg/250px-Stonewall_Inn_5_pride_weekend_2016.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stonewall_Inn_5_pride_weekend_2016.jpg)

[Stonewall Inn](/wiki/Stonewall_Inn "Stonewall Inn") in [Greenwich Village](/wiki/Greenwich_Village "Greenwich Village"), site of the June 1969 [Stonewall riots](/wiki/Stonewall_riots "Stonewall riots"), the cradle of the modern [LGBT rights movement](/wiki/LGBT_social_movements "LGBT social movements")[[196]](#cite_note-GayGreenwichVillage1-200)[[134]](#cite_note-NYTRosenberg20160624-137)[[135]](#cite_note-NPSStonewallMonument-138)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Capital_Gay_Pride_parade_in_Albany_New_York_2009.jpg/250px-Capital_Gay_Pride_parade_in_Albany_New_York_2009.jpg)](/wiki/File:Capital_Gay_Pride_parade_in_Albany_New_York_2009.jpg)

The Capital Gay Pride Parade and Festival in [Albany](/wiki/Albany,_New_York "Albany, New York"), the largest celebration of [LGBTQ+ culture](/wiki/LGBT_history_in_New_York "LGBT history in New York") in [Upstate New York](/wiki/Upstate_New_York "Upstate New York")

In 2013, roughly 3.8 percent of the state's adult population self-identified as [lesbian](/wiki/Lesbian "Lesbian"), [gay](/wiki/Gay "Gay"), [bisexual](/wiki/Bisexual "Bisexual"), or [transgender](/wiki/Transgender "Transgender"), constituting a total LGBT adult population in the state of 570,388 individuals.[[197]](#cite_note-201) In 2010, the number of same-sex couple households stood at roughly 48,932.[[198]](#cite_note-202) New York legalized [same-sex marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_in_New_York "Same-sex marriage in New York") on July 24, 2011; one of the first U.S. states to have done so.[[199]](#cite_note-203)

[New York City](/wiki/LGBT_culture_in_New_York_City "LGBT culture in New York City") has been described as the [gay capital](/wiki/List_of_largest_LGBT_events "List of largest LGBT events") of the world and the central node of the LGBTQ+ [sociopolitical ecosystem](/wiki/Political_sociology "Political sociology"), and is home to one of the world's largest LGBTQ populations and the most prominent.[[200]](#cite_note-NYCGayCapitalOfTheWorld1-204) In July 2012, [Michael Bloomberg](/wiki/Michael_Bloomberg "Michael Bloomberg"), [Mayor of New York City](/wiki/Mayor_of_New_York_City "Mayor of New York City") from 2002 to 2013, said "same-sex marriages in New York City had generated an estimated $259 million in economic impact and $16 million in City revenues" in the first year after enactment of the Marriage Equality Act.[[201]](#cite_note-205) New York City is home to the nation's largest [transgender](/wiki/Transgender "Transgender") population, estimated at 25,000 as of 2016.[[202]](#cite_note-206) The annual [NYC Pride March](/wiki/NYC_Pride_March "NYC Pride March"), the largest pride parade in [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America") held annually in June, traverses down [Fifth Avenue](/wiki/Fifth_Avenue "Fifth Avenue") in Manhattan and ends in [Greenwich Village](/wiki/Greenwich_Village "Greenwich Village").[[203]](#cite_note-NYCWorld'sLargestPrideParade-207)

#### Stonewall riots

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Stonewall riots")]

Main article: [Stonewall riots](/wiki/Stonewall_riots "Stonewall riots")

On June 29, 1969, the [Stonewall riots](/wiki/Stonewall_riots "Stonewall riots") were a series of spontaneous, violent protests by members of the [gay community](/wiki/LGBT_community "LGBT community") against a [police raid](/wiki/Police_raid "Police raid") at the [Stonewall Inn](/wiki/Stonewall_Inn "Stonewall Inn") in [Greenwich Village](/wiki/Greenwich_Village "Greenwich Village"). They are considered to constitute to be one the most important events leading to the [gay liberation](/wiki/Gay_liberation "Gay liberation") movement,[[196]](#cite_note-GayGreenwichVillage1-200)[[204]](#cite_note-KentuckyStonewall-208)[[205]](#cite_note-PinkNewsStonewall-209)[[206]](#cite_note-EncycloStonewall-210) and the modern LGBT rights movement.[[207]](#cite_note-NPSStonewall-211)[[208]](#cite_note-ObamaStonewall-212)

The [Stonewall National Monument](/wiki/Stonewall_National_Monument "Stonewall National Monument"), a [national monument](/wiki/National_monument_(United_States) "National monument (United States)"), commemorates the Stonewall riots. In June 2017, plans were announced for the first monument to LGBT individuals commissioned by the State of New York and planned to be built in [Hudson River Park](/wiki/Hudson_River_Park "Hudson River Park") in Manhattan, near the Hudson River waterfront.[[209]](#cite_note-NYSOfficialLGBTMonument-213)[[210]](#cite_note-214)

[Stonewall 50 – WorldPride NYC 2019](/wiki/Stonewall_50_%E2%80%93_WorldPride_NYC_2019 "Stonewall 50 – WorldPride NYC 2019") commemorated the 50th anniversary of the Stonewall Riots and was the [largest LGBTQ+ pride event](/wiki/List_of_largest_LGBT_events "List of largest LGBT events") in world history, attracting four million attendees in New York City.[[211]](#cite_note-215) The Brooklyn Liberation March, the largest [transgender-rights](/wiki/Legal_status_of_transgender_people "Legal status of transgender people") demonstration in LGBTQ history, took place on June 14, 2020, stretching from [Grand Army Plaza](/wiki/Grand_Army_Plaza "Grand Army Plaza") to the [Fort Greene](/wiki/Fort_Greene,_Brooklyn "Fort Greene, Brooklyn") section of Brooklyn, focused on supporting Black transgender lives, drawing an estimated 15,000 to 20,000 participants.[[212]](#cite_note-216)[[213]](#cite_note-217)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Religion")]

Religious self-identification, per [Public Religion Research Institute](/wiki/Public_Religion_Research_Institute "Public Religion Research Institute")'s 2022 *American Values Survey*[[214]](#cite_note-218)

1. [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholicism_in_the_United_States "Catholicism in the United States") (33.0%)
2. [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism_in_the_United_States "Protestantism in the United States") (27.0%)
3. [Unitarian](/wiki/Unitarianism "Unitarianism")/[Universalist](/wiki/Unitarian_Universalism "Unitarian Universalism") (1.00%)
4. [Jehovah's Witness](/wiki/Jehovah%27s_Witnesses "Jehovah's Witnesses") (1.00%)
5. [Unaffiliated](/wiki/Irreligion_in_the_United_States "Irreligion in the United States") (26.0%)
6. [Judaism](/wiki/American_Jews "American Jews") (7.00%)
7. [New Age](/wiki/New_Age "New Age") (2.00%)
8. [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism_in_the_United_States "Buddhism in the United States") (1.00%)
9. [Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_the_United_States "Islam in the United States") (1.00%)
10. [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism_in_the_United_States "Hinduism in the United States") (1.00%)

In 2014, the [Pew Research Center](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center") released a study of New York's religious population, which found that a majority, 60%, were [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"). Christians were followed by the irreligious (27%), [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") (7%), [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") (2%), [Buddhism and Hinduism](/wiki/Eastern_religions "Eastern religions") (1% each), and other faiths (0.5%).[[215]](#cite_note-Pew-2020-219) Through another study by the [Public Religion Research Institute](/wiki/Public_Religion_Research_Institute "Public Religion Research Institute") in 2020, the majority of New York's religious or spiritual population were 67% Christian, followed by the irreligious (22%), Judaism (4%), Islam (2%), Buddhism and Hinduism (1% each), and other faiths (1%).[[216]](#cite_note-220)

Prior to the 1800s, [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") denominations dominated the religious life of New York, although religion did not play as large a role in the public life of colonial-era [New Netherland](/wiki/New_Netherland "New Netherland") as it did in [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England"), with its Puritan population.[[217]](#cite_note-www.city-data.com-221) Historically, New York served as the foundation for [new Christian denominations](/wiki/Burned-over_district "Burned-over district") in the [Second Great Awakening](/wiki/Second_Great_Awakening "Second Great Awakening"). Non-Western Christian traditions and non-Christian religions did not grow for much of the state's history because immigration was predominantly from [Western Europe](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe") (favored by the quotas in federal immigration law). The [Immigration and Nationality Act of 1965](/wiki/Immigration_and_Nationality_Act_of_1965 "Immigration and Nationality Act of 1965") removed the quotas, allowing for the growth of other religious groups.

The [Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") was the largest Christian denomination in New York as of 2014's study (31%). The largest Roman Catholic diocese was the [Latin Church](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church")'s [Archdiocese of New York](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_New_York "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of New York"). The largest Eastern Catholic diocese was the [Ruthenian Catholic Eparchy of Passaic](/wiki/Ruthenian_Catholic_Eparchy_of_Passaic "Ruthenian Catholic Eparchy of Passaic") of the [Ruthenian Greek Catholic Church](/wiki/Ruthenian_Greek_Catholic_Church "Ruthenian Greek Catholic Church"). The [United Methodist Church](/wiki/United_Methodist_Church "United Methodist Church") was the largest [Mainline Protestant](/wiki/Mainline_Protestant "Mainline Protestant") denomination and second-largest overall, followed by the [Episcopal Church in the U.S.](/wiki/Episcopal_Church_(United_States) "Episcopal Church (United States)") and other [Continuing Anglican](/wiki/Continuing_Anglican_movement "Continuing Anglican movement") bodies. The [Presbyterian Church (USA)](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_(USA) "Presbyterian Church (USA)"), [Evangelical Lutheran Church in America](/wiki/Evangelical_Lutheran_Church_in_America "Evangelical Lutheran Church in America"), and [American Baptist Churches USA](/wiki/American_Baptist_Churches_USA "American Baptist Churches USA") were the following largest Mainline denominations. Mainline Protestants together made up 11% of Christians in the state as of 2014.[[215]](#cite_note-Pew-2020-219) In Evangelical Protestantism, the [Baptists](/wiki/Baptists "Baptists"), [non-denominational Protestants](/wiki/Nondenominational_Christianity "Nondenominational Christianity"), and [Pentecostals](/wiki/Pentecostalism "Pentecostalism") were the largest groups. The [National Baptist Convention (USA)](/wiki/National_Baptist_Convention,_USA,_Inc. "National Baptist Convention, USA, Inc.") and [Progressive National Baptist Convention](/wiki/Progressive_National_Baptist_Convention "Progressive National Baptist Convention") were the largest historically black Protestant churches in New York. Roughly 10% of Christians in New York identified as Evangelical Protestants as of 2014.[[215]](#cite_note-Pew-2020-219) Additionally, the [Eastern](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") and [Oriental Orthodox](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodox_Churches "Oriental Orthodox Churches") collectively comprised 1% of the religious demographic alongside [Jehovah's Witnesses](/wiki/Jehovah%27s_Witnesses "Jehovah's Witnesses") and [other Christians](/wiki/List_of_Christian_denominations "List of Christian denominations"); the Orthodox Christians in 2020's study made up 1% of the population, and Jehovah's Witnesses grew to 1% of the population as well.

According to the Pew Research Center, non-Christian religions accounted for 12% of New York state's population in 2020.[[215]](#cite_note-Pew-2020-219) Judaism was the second-largest religion as of 2014 and 2020. In 2010, 588,500 practiced [Orthodox Judaism](/wiki/Orthodox_Judaism "Orthodox Judaism").[[218]](#cite_note-222) A little over 392,953 professed Islam. The [Powers Street Mosque](/wiki/Powers_Street_Mosque "Powers Street Mosque") in New York City was the state's first Muslim organization.[[219]](#cite_note-223) New York is also home to the oldest [Zoroastrian](/wiki/Zoroastrian "Zoroastrian") [fire temple](/wiki/Fire_temple "Fire temple") in the nation.[[220]](#cite_note-224) Less than 1% of New York's population practiced [New Age](/wiki/New_Age "New Age") and [contemporary paganism](/wiki/Modern_Paganism "Modern Paganism"). [Native American religions](/wiki/Native_American_religion "Native American religion") were also a minority religion.[[215]](#cite_note-Pew-2020-219) Statewide, 17% were not religiously practicing, 5% identified as agnostic, and 5% as [atheist](/wiki/Atheist "Atheist").

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Economy")]

Main article: [Economy of New York (state)](/wiki/Economy_of_New_York_(state) "Economy of New York (state)")

See also: [New York locations by per capita income](/wiki/New_York_locations_by_per_capita_income "New York locations by per capita income") and [Economy of New York City](/wiki/Economy_of_New_York_City "Economy of New York City")

New York's [gross domestic product](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") (GDP) in 2022-Q2 was US$2.0 trillion.[[221]](#cite_note-GDPByState-225) If the State of New York were an independent nation, it would rank as the 11th-largest economy in the world.[[222]](#cite_note-226) However, in 2022, the multi-state, New York City-centered [metropolitan statistical area](/wiki/List_of_Metropolitan_Statistical_Areas "List of Metropolitan Statistical Areas") produced a [gross metropolitan product](/wiki/Gross_metropolitan_product "Gross metropolitan product") (GMP) of over US$2.16 trillion, [the largest metropolitan economy worldwide](/wiki/List_of_cities_by_GDP "List of cities by GDP") and behind the [GDP](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") of only nine nations.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In 2025, 99.8% of New York businesses were [small businesses](/wiki/Small_business "Small business") and employed 46.6% of the state's work force, or 3.9 million people, according to the [U.S. Small Business Administration](/wiki/Small_Business_Administration "Small Business Administration") Office of Advocacy.[[223]](#cite_note-227) Over 267,000 new businesses formed within the state in 2025.[[224]](#cite_note-228)

### Wall Street

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Wall Street")]

Main article: [Wall Street](/wiki/Wall_Street "Wall Street")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/NYC_NYSE.jpg/250px-NYC_NYSE.jpg)](/wiki/File:NYC_NYSE.jpg)

The [New York Stock Exchange](/wiki/New_York_Stock_Exchange "New York Stock Exchange"), the world's largest [stock exchange](/wiki/Stock_exchange "Stock exchange") by total [market capitalization](/wiki/Market_capitalization "Market capitalization") of its listed companies[[225]](#cite_note-NYSEhighestcap-229)

Anchored by Wall Street in the [Financial District](/wiki/Financial_District,_Manhattan "Financial District, Manhattan") of [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan"), New York City has been called both the most economically powerful city and the leading financial center of the world.[[32]](#cite_note-WorldEconomicAndFinancialSuperCenter-35)[[226]](#cite_note-EconomicallyPowerful2015-230)[[227]](#cite_note-231)[[228]](#cite_note-232)[[229]](#cite_note-233) Lower Manhattan is the third-largest [central business district](/wiki/Central_business_district "Central business district") in the United States and is home to the [New York Stock Exchange](/wiki/New_York_Stock_Exchange "New York Stock Exchange"), on Wall Street, and [Nasdaq](/wiki/Nasdaq "Nasdaq"), at [165 Broadway](/wiki/One_Liberty_Plaza "One Liberty Plaza"), representing the world's largest and second-largest [stock exchanges](/wiki/List_of_stock_exchanges "List of stock exchanges"), respectively, as measured both by overall average daily trading volume and by total [market capitalization](/wiki/Market_capitalization "Market capitalization") of their listed companies in 2023.

New York City remains the largest global center for trading in [public equity](/wiki/Public_equity "Public equity") and [debt](/wiki/Security_(finance) "Security (finance)") [capital markets](/wiki/Capital_markets "Capital markets"), driven in part by the size and [financial development](/wiki/Financial_Development_Index "Financial Development Index") of the [U.S. economy](/wiki/U.S._economy "U.S. economy").[[230]](#cite_note-CNY2007-234): 31–32[[231]](#cite_note-235) New York also leads in [private equity](/wiki/List_of_private_equity_firms "List of private equity firms") and the monetary volume of [mergers and acquisitions](/wiki/Mergers_and_acquisitions "Mergers and acquisitions"). Several financial institutions and related managers based in Manhattan are important participants in other global financial centers.[[230]](#cite_note-CNY2007-234): 34–35 New York is also the principal [commercial banking](/wiki/Commercial_banking "Commercial banking") center of the United States.[[232]](#cite_note-236)

Many of the world's largest [media conglomerates](/wiki/Media_conglomerate "Media conglomerate") are also based in the city. [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") contained approximately 520 million square feet (48.1 million m2) of office space in 2013,[[233]](#cite_note-237) making it the largest office market in the United States,[[234]](#cite_note-238) while [Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan") is the largest central business district in the world.[[235]](#cite_note-239)

### High technology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: High technology")]

#### Silicon Alley eastward throughout Long Island

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Silicon Alley eastward throughout Long Island")]

Main article: [Silicon Alley](/wiki/Silicon_Alley "Silicon Alley")

Further information: [Tech:NYC](/wiki/Tech:NYC "Tech:NYC"), [Tech companies in New York](/wiki/Tech_companies_in_New_York "Tech companies in New York"), and [Biotech and pharmaceutical companies in New York](/wiki/Biotech_and_pharmaceutical_companies_in_New_York "Biotech and pharmaceutical companies in New York")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Cold_Spring_Harbor_Laboratory.jpg/250px-Cold_Spring_Harbor_Laboratory.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cold_Spring_Harbor_Laboratory.jpg)

[Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory](/wiki/Cold_Spring_Harbor_Laboratory "Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory") on the [North Shore](/wiki/North_Shore_(Long_Island) "North Shore (Long Island)") of [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island"), an internationally renowned [biomedical research](/wiki/Biomedical_research "Biomedical research") facility and home to eight scientists awarded the [Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physiology_or_Medicine "Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine")

[Silicon Alley](/wiki/Silicon_Alley "Silicon Alley"), once confined to [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan"), evolved into a [metonym](/wiki/Metonym "Metonym") for the sphere encompassing the New York City metropolitan region's [high technology and entrepreneurship ecosystem](/wiki/Entrepreneurship_ecosystem "Entrepreneurship ecosystem"). [High tech](/wiki/High_tech "High tech") industries including [digital media](/wiki/Digital_media "Digital media"), [biotechnology](/wiki/Biotechnology "Biotechnology"), [software development](/wiki/Software_development "Software development"), [game design](/wiki/Game_design "Game design"), and other fields in [information technology](/wiki/Information_technology "Information technology") are growing, bolstered by New York City's position at the terminus of several [transatlantic fiber optic trunk lines](/wiki/Transatlantic_telephone_cable "Transatlantic telephone cable"),[[236]](#cite_note-240) its [intellectual capital](/wiki/Intellectual_capital "Intellectual capital"), as well as its growing outdoor [wireless connectivity](/wiki/Wireless_network "Wireless network").[[237]](#cite_note-241)

In December 2014, the State of New York announced a US$50 million venture-capital fund to encourage enterprises working in biotechnology and [advanced materials](/wiki/Materials_science "Materials science"); according to former Governor [Andrew Cuomo](/wiki/Andrew_Cuomo "Andrew Cuomo"), the [seed money](/wiki/Seed_money "Seed money") would facilitate [entrepreneurs](/wiki/Entrepreneur "Entrepreneur") in bringing their research into the marketplace.[[238]](#cite_note-242)

On December 19, 2011, then Mayor Michael R. Bloomberg announced his choice of [Cornell University](/wiki/Cornell_University "Cornell University") and [Technion-Israel Institute of Technology](/wiki/Technion-Israel_Institute_of_Technology "Technion-Israel Institute of Technology") to build a two billion dollar [graduate school](/wiki/Graduate_school "Graduate school") of [applied sciences](/wiki/Applied_science "Applied science") on [Roosevelt Island](/wiki/Roosevelt_Island "Roosevelt Island") in Manhattan, with the goal of transforming New York City into the world's premier technology capital.[[239]](#cite_note-243)[[240]](#cite_note-244)

New York City's [artificial intelligence](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence "Artificial intelligence") (AI) sector alone raised US$483.6 million in venture capital investment in 2022.[[241]](#cite_note-NYCArtificialIntelligenceHub-245) In 2023, New York unveiled the first comprehensive initiative to create both a framework of rules and a [chatbot](/wiki/Chatbot "Chatbot") to regulate the use of AI within the sphere of city government.[[242]](#cite_note-NYCArtificialIntelligenceGovernment-246)

[Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island") is a prominent nexus for [STEM](/wiki/STEM "STEM")-based education and technology. [Biotechnology companies](/wiki/Biotech_and_pharmaceutical_companies_in_the_New_York_metropolitan_area "Biotech and pharmaceutical companies in the New York metropolitan area") and [scientific research](/wiki/Scientific_research "Scientific research") play a significant role in Long Island's economy,[[243]](#cite_note-LongIslandBioPharma-247) including [research facilities](/wiki/Research_institute "Research institute") at [Brookhaven National Laboratory](/wiki/Brookhaven_National_Laboratory "Brookhaven National Laboratory"), [Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory](/wiki/Cold_Spring_Harbor_Laboratory "Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory"), [Stony Brook University](/wiki/Stony_Brook_University "Stony Brook University"), [New York Institute of Technology](/wiki/New_York_Institute_of_Technology "New York Institute of Technology"), [Plum Island Animal Disease Center](/wiki/Plum_Island_Animal_Disease_Center "Plum Island Animal Disease Center"), the [New York University Tandon School of Engineering](/wiki/New_York_University_Tandon_School_of_Engineering "New York University Tandon School of Engineering"), the [City University of New York](/wiki/City_University_of_New_York "City University of New York"), the [Hofstra Northwell School of Medicine](/wiki/Zucker_School_of_Medicine "Zucker School of Medicine"), and the [Feinstein Institutes for Medical Research](/wiki/Feinstein_Institutes_for_Medical_Research "Feinstein Institutes for Medical Research").

#### Tech Valley

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Tech Valley")]

Main article: [Tech Valley](/wiki/Tech_Valley "Tech Valley")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/IBM_Yorktown_Heights.jpg/250px-IBM_Yorktown_Heights.jpg)](/wiki/File:IBM_Yorktown_Heights.jpg)

The main laboratory building of the [IBM](/wiki/IBM_Research "IBM Research") [Watson Research Center](/wiki/Thomas_J._Watson_Research_Center "Thomas J. Watson Research Center") in [Yorktown Heights](/wiki/Yorktown_Heights,_New_York "Yorktown Heights, New York")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/1_times_square_night_2013.jpg/250px-1_times_square_night_2013.jpg)](/wiki/File:1_times_square_night_2013.jpg)

[Times Square](/wiki/Times_Square "Times Square") in [Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan"), hub of the [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_theatre "Broadway theatre")'s [theater district](/wiki/Theater_District_(Manhattan) "Theater District (Manhattan)"), a [media](/wiki/Media_(communication) "Media (communication)") center, and one of the world's busiest [pedestrian](/wiki/Pedestrian "Pedestrian") intersections

[Albany](/wiki/Albany,_New_York "Albany, New York"),[[244]](#cite_note-MadeInAlbanyNY-248) [Saratoga County](/wiki/Saratoga_County,_New_York "Saratoga County, New York"),[[245]](#cite_note-MadeInSaratogaCountyNY-249)[[246]](#cite_note-250) [Rensselaer County](/wiki/Rensselaer_County,_New_York "Rensselaer County, New York"), and the [Hudson Valley](/wiki/Hudson_Valley "Hudson Valley"), collectively recognized as eastern New York's [Tech Valley](/wiki/Tech_Valley "Tech Valley"), have experienced significant growth in the [computer hardware](/wiki/Computer_hardware "Computer hardware") ecosystem within the [high-technology industry](/wiki/High-technology_industry "High-technology industry"), making great strides in the [nanotechnology](/wiki/Nanotechnology "Nanotechnology") sector, [digital electronics](/wiki/Digital_electronics "Digital electronics") design, and water- and electricity-dependent [integrated microchip circuit](/wiki/Integrated_circuit "Integrated circuit") manufacturing,[[245]](#cite_note-MadeInSaratogaCountyNY-249) involving companies including [IBM](/wiki/IBM "IBM") and its [Thomas J. Watson Research Center](/wiki/Thomas_J._Watson_Research_Center "Thomas J. Watson Research Center"),[[247]](#cite_note-251) and the three foreign-owned firms, GlobalFoundries, [Samsung](/wiki/Samsung "Samsung"), and [Taiwan Semiconductor](/wiki/Taiwan_Semiconductor_Manufacturing_Company_Limited "Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited"), among others.[[244]](#cite_note-MadeInAlbanyNY-248)[[248]](#cite_note-252) The area's high technology ecosystem is supported by technologically focused [academic institutions](/wiki/Academic_institution "Academic institution") including [Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute](/wiki/Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute "Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute") and the [SUNY Polytechnic Institute](/wiki/State_University_of_New_York_Polytechnic_Institute "State University of New York Polytechnic Institute").[[244]](#cite_note-MadeInAlbanyNY-248)

In 2015, Tech Valley, straddling both sides of the [Adirondack Northway](/wiki/Adirondack_Northway "Adirondack Northway") and the [New York Thruway](/wiki/New_York_Thruway "New York Thruway"), generated over $163 million in venture capital investment.[[17]](#cite_note-VentureCapitalNY1-20) The [Rochester](/wiki/Rochester,_New_York "Rochester, New York") area is important in the field of [photographic processing and imaging](/wiki/Photographic_processing "Photographic processing") as well as [incubating](/wiki/Business_incubator "Business incubator") an increasingly diverse high technology sphere encompassing [STEM fields](/wiki/STEM_fields "STEM fields"), similarly in part the result of private [startup enterprises](/wiki/Startup_company "Startup company") collaborating with major academic institutions, including the [University of Rochester](/wiki/University_of_Rochester "University of Rochester") and Cornell University.[[249]](#cite_note-253)

[Westchester County](/wiki/Westchester_County,_New_York "Westchester County, New York") has developed a burgeoning [biotechnology sector](/wiki/Biotechnology "Biotechnology") in the 21st century, with over a billion dollars in planned private investment as of 2016.[[250]](#cite_note-254)[[251]](#cite_note-255) In April 2021, [GlobalFoundries](/wiki/GlobalFoundries "GlobalFoundries"), a company specializing in the [semiconductor industry](/wiki/Semiconductor_industry "Semiconductor industry"), moved its headquarters from [Silicon Valley, California](/wiki/Silicon_Valley,_California "Silicon Valley, California") to its most advanced [semiconductor-chip manufacturing](/wiki/Semiconductor "Semiconductor") facility in [Saratoga County](/wiki/Saratoga_County,_New_York "Saratoga County, New York") near a section of the Adirondack Northway, in [Malta, New York](/wiki/Malta,_New_York "Malta, New York").[[252]](#cite_note-GlobalFoundriesSaratogaCountyHeadquartersNewYork-256)

### Media and entertainment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Media and entertainment")]

Main article: [Media in New York City](/wiki/Media_in_New_York_City "Media in New York City")

[Creative industries](/wiki/Creative_industries "Creative industries"), which are concerned with generating and distributing [knowledge](/wiki/Knowledge "Knowledge") and [information](/wiki/Information "Information"), such as new media, digital media, [film](/wiki/Film_production "Film production") and [television production](/wiki/Television_production "Television production"), advertising, fashion, design, and architecture, account for a growing share of employment, with New York City possessing a strong competitive advantage in these industries.[[253]](#cite_note-257) As of 2014[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit), the State of New York was offering tax incentives of up to $420 million annually for [filmmaking](/wiki/Filmmaking "Filmmaking") within the state, the most generous such [tax rebate](/wiki/Tax_rebate "Tax rebate") among U.S. states. New York has also attracted higher-wage [visual-effects](/wiki/Visual_effects "Visual effects") employment by further augmenting its tax credit to a maximum of 35% for performing [post-film production](/wiki/Post-production "Post-production") work in Upstate New York.[[254]](#cite_note-258) The filmed [entertainment industry](/wiki/Entertainment_industry "Entertainment industry") has been growing in New York, contributing nearly $9 billion to the New York City economy alone as of 2015.[[255]](#cite_note-259)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/I_Love_New_York.svg/250px-I_Love_New_York.svg.png)](/wiki/File:I_Love_New_York.svg)
