### Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Tourism")]

Main articles: [Tourism in New York City](/wiki/Tourism_in_New_York_City "Tourism in New York City"), [Niagara Falls](/wiki/Niagara_Falls "Niagara Falls"), and [Broadway theatre](/wiki/Broadway_theatre "Broadway theatre")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Container_Ship_CMA_CGM_Theodore_Roosevelt_in_NY_Harbor_on_Sept_7%2C_2017_%2836415318053%29.jpg/250px-Container_Ship_CMA_CGM_Theodore_Roosevelt_in_NY_Harbor_on_Sept_7%2C_2017_%2836415318053%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Container_Ship_CMA_CGM_Theodore_Roosevelt_in_NY_Harbor_on_Sept_7,_2017_(36415318053).jpg)

The *Theodore Roosevelt*, the largest container ship to enter the [Port of New York and New Jersey](/wiki/Port_of_New_York_and_New_Jersey "Port of New York and New Jersey") as of September 2017

[I Love New York](/wiki/I_Love_New_York "I Love New York") (stylized as I ❤ NY) is a [slogan](/wiki/Slogan "Slogan"), a [logo](/wiki/Logo "Logo"), and state song that are the basis of an [advertising](/wiki/Advertising "Advertising") campaign used since 1977 to promote [tourism](/wiki/Tourism "Tourism") in the New York state,[[256]](#cite_note-ny_logo-260) including [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City").[[257]](#cite_note-261)[[258]](#cite_note-262) The [trademarked](/wiki/Trademarked "Trademarked") logo is owned by [New York State Empire State Development](/wiki/Empire_State_Development_Corporation "Empire State Development Corporation").[[256]](#cite_note-ny_logo-260) By 2025, tourism had become New York's second-largest industry (after [finance](/wiki/Financial_center "Financial center")), supporting one in 10 jobs and generating billions of dollars in economic impact annually.[[259]](#cite_note-NewYorkTourismSecondLargestIndustry-263) [The Broadway League](/wiki/The_Broadway_League "The Broadway League") reported that [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_theatre "Broadway theatre") shows sold approximately $1.27 billion worth of tickets in the 2013–2014 season, an 11.4% increase from $1.139 billion in the 2012–2013 season. Attendance in 2013–2014 stood at 12.21 million, representing a 5.5% increase from the 2012–2013 season's 11.57 million.[[260]](#cite_note-league-264)

### Exports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Exports")]

New York exports a wide variety of goods such as prepared foods, [computers](/wiki/Computer "Computer"), and [electronics](/wiki/Consumer_electronics "Consumer electronics"), cut [diamonds](/wiki/Diamond "Diamond"), and other commodities. New York's largest imports are oil, gold, aluminum, natural gas, electricity, rough diamonds, and lumber. The state also has a large [manufacturing sector](/wiki/Manufacturing_sector "Manufacturing sector") that includes printing and the production of [garments](/wiki/Garment "Garment"), mainly in New York City; and furs, railroad equipment, automobile parts, and bus line vehicles, concentrated in Upstate regions.

New York is the nation's third-largest [grape](/wiki/Grape "Grape") producing state, and third-largest [wine](/wiki/New_York_wine "New York wine") producer by volume, behind California and Washington. The southern [Finger Lakes](/wiki/Finger_Lakes "Finger Lakes") hillsides, the Hudson Valley, the [North Fork](/wiki/North_Fork,_Suffolk_County,_New_York "North Fork, Suffolk County, New York") of Long Island, and the southern shore of Lake Erie are the primary grape- and wine-growing regions in New York, with many [vineyards](/wiki/Vineyard "Vineyard"). In 2012, New York had 320 [wineries](/wiki/Wineries "Wineries") and 37,000 grape bearing acres (15,000 ha), generating full-time employment for nearly 25,000 and annual wages over $1.1 billion, and yielding $4.8 billion in direct economic impact from New York grapes, grape juice, and wine and grape products.[[261]](#cite_note-265)

### Agriculture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Agriculture")]

The [New York agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture_in_New_York "Agriculture in New York") industry is a major producer overall, ranking among the top five states for agricultural products including [maple syrup](/wiki/Maple_syrup "Maple syrup"), apples, cherries, cabbage, [dairy products](/wiki/New_York_dairy_industry "New York dairy industry"), onions, and potatoes. The state is the largest producer of cabbage in the U.S. The state has about a quarter of its land in farms and produced $3.4 billion in agricultural products in 2001. The south shore of Lake Ontario provides the right mix of soils and [microclimate](/wiki/Microclimate "Microclimate") for many apple, cherry, plum, pear and peach orchards. Apples are also grown in the Hudson Valley and near Lake Champlain. A moderately sized saltwater commercial fishery is located along the Atlantic side of Long Island. The principal catches by value are clams, lobsters, squid, and [flounder](/wiki/Flounder "Flounder").[[262]](#cite_note-266)

### Energy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Energy")]

Further information: [New York energy law](/wiki/New_York_energy_law "New York energy law"), [Solar power in New York](/wiki/Solar_power_in_New_York "Solar power in New York"), and [List of power stations in New York](/wiki/List_of_power_stations_in_New_York "List of power stations in New York")

In 2017, the State of New York consumed 156,370 [gigawatthours](/wiki/Kilowatt_hour "Kilowatt hour") (GWh) of electrical energy. Downstate regions (Hudson Valley, New York City, and Long Island) consumed 66% of that amount. Upstate regions produced 50% of that amount. The peak load in 2017 was 29,699 MW. The resource capability in 2017 was 42,839 MW.[[263]](#cite_note-267)[[264]](#cite_note-268) The [NYISO's](/wiki/New_York_energy_law#NYISO "New York energy law") market monitor described the average all-in wholesale electric price as a range (a single value was not provided) from $25 per MWh to $53 per MWh for 2017.[[265]](#cite_note-269)

### Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Transportation")]

Main article: [Transportation in New York (state)](/wiki/Transportation_in_New_York_(state) "Transportation in New York (state)")

[![A subway train and many people are seen in New York City's subway system.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/R188_Subway_Car%2C_7898%2C_7%2C_September_5th%2C_2014.jpg/250px-R188_Subway_Car%2C_7898%2C_7%2C_September_5th%2C_2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:R188_Subway_Car,_7898,_7,_September_5th,_2014.jpg)

The [New York City Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway"), [one of the world's busiest subway systems](/wiki/List_of_metro_systems "List of metro systems"), serving over five million passengers each weekday

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Grand_Central_Terminal_ceiling_view.jpg/250px-Grand_Central_Terminal_ceiling_view.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grand_Central_Terminal_ceiling_view.jpg)

[Grand Central Terminal](/wiki/Grand_Central_Terminal "Grand Central Terminal") in [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan")

[![Five jumbo airplanes wait in a line on a runway next to a small body of water at John F. Kennedy Airport.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/JFK_Plane_Queue.jpg/250px-JFK_Plane_Queue.jpg)](/wiki/File:JFK_Plane_Queue.jpg)

[John F. Kennedy International Airport](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_International_Airport "John F. Kennedy International Airport") in [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens"), the [busiest international air passenger gateway](/wiki/Busiest_airports_in_the_United_States_by_international_passenger_traffic "Busiest airports in the United States by international passenger traffic") to the United States

New York has one of the oldest and most extensive transportation infrastructures in the country. Engineering challenges posed by the complex terrain of the state and the unique infrastructural issues of New York City brought on by urban crowding have had to be overcome perennially. Population expansion of the state has followed the path of the early waterways, first the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River") and [Mohawk River](/wiki/Mohawk_River "Mohawk River"), then the [Erie Canal](/wiki/Erie_Canal "Erie Canal"). In the 19th century, railroads were constructed along the river valleys, followed by the [New York State Thruway](/wiki/New_York_State_Thruway "New York State Thruway") in the 20th century.

#### Roads and highways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Roads and highways")]

The [New York State Department of Transportation](/wiki/New_York_State_Department_of_Transportation "New York State Department of Transportation") (NYSDOT) is the department of the [government of New York](/wiki/Government_of_New_York_(state) "Government of New York (state)") responsible for the development and operation of [highways](/wiki/Numbered_highways_in_New_York "Numbered highways in New York"), [railroads](/wiki/List_of_New_York_railroads "List of New York railroads"), [mass transit](/wiki/Mass_transit "Mass transit") systems, [ports](/wiki/Port "Port"), [waterways](/wiki/Waterways "Waterways"), and [aviation facilities](/wiki/List_of_airports_in_New_York_(state) "List of airports in New York (state)") within the State of New York.[[266]](#cite_note-270) The NYSDOT is headquartered at 50 Wolf Road in [Colonie](/wiki/Colonie,_New_York "Colonie, New York"), [Albany County](/wiki/Albany_County,_New_York "Albany County, New York"). The [Port Authority of New York and New Jersey](/wiki/Port_Authority_of_New_York_and_New_Jersey "Port Authority of New York and New Jersey") (PANYNJ) is a joint venture between the states of New York and New Jersey and authorized by the [U.S. Congress](/wiki/U.S._Congress "U.S. Congress"), established in 1921 through an [interstate compact](/wiki/Interstate_compact "Interstate compact"), that oversees much of the regional transportation infrastructure, including [bridges](/wiki/Bridge "Bridge"), [tunnels](/wiki/Tunnel "Tunnel"), [airports](/wiki/Airport "Airport"), and [seaports](/wiki/Seaport "Seaport"), within the geographical jurisdiction of the [Port of New York and New Jersey](/wiki/Port_of_New_York_and_New_Jersey "Port of New York and New Jersey"). This 1,500 sq mi (3,900 km2) port district is generally encompassed within a 25 mi (40 km) radius of the [Statue of Liberty National Monument](/wiki/Statue_of_Liberty_National_Monument "Statue of Liberty National Monument").[[267]](#cite_note-PANYNJ-annual-report-2002-271) The Port Authority is headquartered at [4 World Trade Center](/wiki/4_World_Trade_Center "4 World Trade Center") in Lower Manhattan.

The [New York State Department of Motor Vehicles](/wiki/New_York_State_Department_of_Motor_Vehicles "New York State Department of Motor Vehicles") (NYSDMV or DMV) is the governmental agency responsible for registering and [inspecting automobiles](/wiki/Vehicle_inspection "Vehicle inspection") and other motor vehicles, as well as licensing drivers in the State of New York. As of 2008[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit), the NYSDMV has 11,284,546 drivers licenses on file and 10,697,644 vehicle registrations in force.[[268]](#cite_note-272)[[269]](#cite_note-273) All gasoline-powered vehicles registered in the State of New York are required to have an [emissions inspection](/wiki/Emission_standard "Emission standard") every 12 months, in order to ensure that [environmental quality](/wiki/Environmental_quality "Environmental quality") controls are working to prevent air pollution. Diesel-powered vehicles with a gross weight rating over 8,500 pounds that are registered in most Downstate New York counties must get an annual emissions inspection. All vehicles registered in the State of New York must get an annual safety inspection.

#### Public transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Public transportation")]

In addition to the [New York City Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway") system, which is confined to the five boroughs of New York City, New York state has four suburban [commuter railroad](/wiki/Commuter_railroad "Commuter railroad") systems that enter and depart the city: the [Long Island Rail Road](/wiki/Long_Island_Rail_Road "Long Island Rail Road"), [Metro-North Railroad](/wiki/Metro-North_Railroad "Metro-North Railroad"), [Port Authority Trans-Hudson](/wiki/Port_Authority_Trans-Hudson "Port Authority Trans-Hudson"), and five of [New Jersey Transit's rail lines](/wiki/New_Jersey_Transit_Rail_Operations "New Jersey Transit Rail Operations"). The [New York City Department of Transportation](/wiki/New_York_City_Department_of_Transportation "New York City Department of Transportation") (NYCDOT) is the agency of the [government of New York City](/wiki/Government_of_New_York_City "Government of New York City") responsible for the management of much of New York City's own transportation infrastructure.[[270]](#cite_note-274) In Buffalo, the [Niagara Frontier Transportation Authority](/wiki/Niagara_Frontier_Transportation_Authority "Niagara Frontier Transportation Authority") runs the [Buffalo Metro Rail](/wiki/Buffalo_Metro_Rail "Buffalo Metro Rail") light-rail system; in Rochester, the [Rochester Subway](/wiki/Rochester_Subway "Rochester Subway") operated from 1927 until 1956, but fell into disuse as state and federal investment went to highways.

#### Airports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Airports")]

Portions of the transportation system are [intermodal](/wiki/Intermodal_passenger_transport "Intermodal passenger transport"), allowing travelers to switch easily from one mode of transportation to another. One of the most notable examples is [AirTrain JFK](/wiki/AirTrain_JFK "AirTrain JFK") which allows rail passengers to travel directly to [terminals](/wiki/Airport_terminal "Airport terminal") at [John F. Kennedy International Airport](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_International_Airport "John F. Kennedy International Airport") and to the underground New York City Subway system.

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Education")]

Main article: [Education in New York (state)](/wiki/Education_in_New_York_(state) "Education in New York (state)")

See also: [List of colleges and universities in New York (state)](/wiki/List_of_colleges_and_universities_in_New_York_(state) "List of colleges and universities in New York (state)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Butler_Library_-_1000px_-_AC.jpg/250px-Butler_Library_-_1000px_-_AC.jpg)](/wiki/File:Butler_Library_-_1000px_-_AC.jpg)

[Butler Library](/wiki/Butler_Library "Butler Library") at [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University"), an [Ivy League](/wiki/Ivy_League "Ivy League") university in [Upper Manhattan](/wiki/Upper_Manhattan "Upper Manhattan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/Harris_Hall_1589_Amst_Av_jeh.jpg/250px-Harris_Hall_1589_Amst_Av_jeh.jpg)](/wiki/File:Harris_Hall_1589_Amst_Av_jeh.jpg)

[Harris Hall](/wiki/Harris_Hall "Harris Hall") at [City College of New York](/wiki/City_College_of_New_York "City College of New York"), a public college of the [City University of New York](/wiki/City_University_of_New_York "City University of New York") in the [Hamilton Heights](/wiki/Hamilton_Heights,_Manhattan "Hamilton Heights, Manhattan") section of [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan")

Among all New York-based colleges and universities, [Cornell University](/wiki/Cornell_University "Cornell University") in [Ithaca](/wiki/Ithaca,_New_York "Ithaca, New York") and [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University") in [Upper Manhattan](/wiki/Upper_Manhattan "Upper Manhattan"), both [Ivy League](/wiki/Ivy_League "Ivy League") universities, are the most selective universities, and both are world-renowned private universities. [New York University](/wiki/New_York_University "New York University") in Manhattan also ranks highly among New York state-based universities. Other notable large private universities include [Syracuse University](/wiki/Syracuse_University "Syracuse University") and [Fordham University](/wiki/Fordham_University "Fordham University"). Smaller notable private institutions of higher education include [University of Rochester](/wiki/University_of_Rochester "University of Rochester"), [Rockefeller University](/wiki/Rockefeller_University "Rockefeller University"), [Mercy University](/wiki/Mercy_University "Mercy University"), [New York Institute of Technology](/wiki/New_York_Institute_of_Technology "New York Institute of Technology"), [Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute](/wiki/Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute "Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute"), [Yeshiva University](/wiki/Yeshiva_University "Yeshiva University"), and [Hofstra University](/wiki/Hofstra_University "Hofstra University"). There are also a multitude of [postgraduate](/wiki/Postgraduate_degree "Postgraduate degree")-level schools in the State of New York, including [medical](/wiki/Medical_School "Medical School"), [law](/wiki/Law_School "Law School"), and [engineering](/wiki/Engineering "Engineering") schools such as [New York Medical College](/wiki/New_York_Medical_College "New York Medical College") and [New York Law School](/wiki/New_York_Law_School "New York Law School").

The [United States Military Academy](/wiki/United_States_Military_Academy "United States Military Academy") at [West Point](/wiki/West_Point,_New_York "West Point, New York"), the service academy of the [U.S. Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army"), is located just south of [Newburgh](/wiki/Newburgh_(city),_New_York "Newburgh (city), New York"), on the west bank of the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River"). The federal [Merchant Marine Academy](/wiki/United_States_Merchant_Marine_Academy "United States Merchant Marine Academy") is at [Kings Point](/wiki/Kings_Point,_New_York "Kings Point, New York") on [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island").

In [post-secondary education](/wiki/Higher_education_in_the_United_States "Higher education in the United States"), the statewide public university system is the [State University of New York](/wiki/State_University_of_New_York "State University of New York") (SUNY). The SUNY system consists of 64 community colleges, technical colleges, undergraduate colleges, and doctoral-granting institutions.[[271]](#cite_note-275) The SUNY system has four "university centers": [Albany](/wiki/University_at_Albany,_SUNY "University at Albany, SUNY") (1844), [Buffalo](/wiki/University_at_Buffalo "University at Buffalo") (1846), [Binghamton](/wiki/Binghamton_University "Binghamton University") (1946), and [Stony Brook](/wiki/Stony_Brook_University "Stony Brook University") (1957), of which Buffalo and Stony Brook are the two flagship universities.[[272]](#cite_note-276) The SUNY system is home to three academic medical centers: [Renaissance School of Medicine at Stony Brook University](/wiki/Renaissance_School_of_Medicine_at_Stony_Brook_University "Renaissance School of Medicine at Stony Brook University") on [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island"), [Norton College of Medicine](/wiki/State_University_of_New_York_Upstate_Medical_University#Norton_College_of_Medicine "State University of New York Upstate Medical University") at [SUNY Upstate Medical University](/wiki/State_University_of_New_York_Upstate_Medical_University "State University of New York Upstate Medical University") in [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_New_York "Syracuse, New York"), and [SUNY Downstate Medical Center](/wiki/SUNY_Downstate_Medical_Center "SUNY Downstate Medical Center") in [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn").

The [City University of New York](/wiki/City_University_of_New_York "City University of New York") is the [public](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") [university system](/wiki/University_system "University system") of [New York City](/wiki/Education_in_New_York_City "Education in New York City"). It is the largest urban university system in the United States, comprising 25 campuses: eleven [senior colleges](/wiki/Upper_division_college "Upper division college"), seven [community colleges](/wiki/Community_college "Community college") and seven professional institutions. While its constituent colleges date back as far as 1847, CUNY was established in 1961. The university enrolls more than 275,000 students, and counts thirteen [Nobel Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Prize "Nobel Prize") winners and twenty-four [MacArthur Fellows](/wiki/MacArthur_Fellows_Program "MacArthur Fellows Program") among its alumni.[[273]](#cite_note-277)

A number of selective private liberal arts institutions are located in New York. Among them are [Adelphi University](/wiki/Adelphi_University "Adelphi University"), [Bard College](/wiki/Bard_College "Bard College"), [Barnard College](/wiki/Barnard_College "Barnard College"), [Colgate University](/wiki/Colgate_University "Colgate University"), [Hamilton College](/wiki/Hamilton_College "Hamilton College"), [Hobart and William Smith Colleges](/wiki/Hobart_and_William_Smith_Colleges "Hobart and William Smith Colleges"), [Marist College](/wiki/Marist_College "Marist College"), [Sarah Lawrence College](/wiki/Sarah_Lawrence_College "Sarah Lawrence College"), [Skidmore College](/wiki/Skidmore_College "Skidmore College"), [St. Lawrence University](/wiki/St._Lawrence_University "St. Lawrence University"), [Union College](/wiki/Union_College "Union College"), and [Vassar College](/wiki/Vassar_College "Vassar College"). Two of these schools, Barnard and Vassar, are members of the selective [Seven Sisters](/wiki/Seven_Sisters_(colleges) "Seven Sisters (colleges)"), originally all women's colleges with ties to the [Ivy League](/wiki/Ivy_League "Ivy League"). Barnard is affiliated with [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University"), its Manhattan neighbor, and Vassar became coeducational in 1969 after declining an offer to merge with [Yale University](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University").

New York is also home to what are widely regarded as the best performing arts schools in the world. The [Juilliard School](/wiki/Juilliard_School "Juilliard School"), located on the [Upper West Side](/wiki/Upper_West_Side "Upper West Side") of [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan"), is one of the world's leading music and dance schools.[[274]](#cite_note-278)[[275]](#cite_note-279)[[276]](#cite_note-280) The [Eastman School of Music](/wiki/Eastman_School_of_Music "Eastman School of Music"), a professional school within the [University of Rochester](/wiki/University_of_Rochester "University of Rochester"), was ranked first among U.S. music schools by *[U.S. News & World Report](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report")* for five consecutive years.[[277]](#cite_note-281)

The [University of the State of New York](/wiki/University_of_the_State_of_New_York "University of the State of New York") accredits and sets standards for elementary, middle-level, and secondary education in the state, while the [New York State Education Department](/wiki/New_York_State_Education_Department "New York State Education Department") oversees public schools and controls their [standardized tests](/wiki/Standardized_test "Standardized test"). The [New York City Department of Education](/wiki/New_York_City_Department_of_Education "New York City Department of Education") manages the [New York City Public Schools](/wiki/New_York_City_Public_Schools "New York City Public Schools") system. In 1894, reflecting general racial discrimination then, the state passed a law that allowed communities to set up separate schools for children of African-American descent. In 1900, the state passed another law requiring integrated schools.[[278]](#cite_note-282) During the 2013 fiscal year, New York spent more on public education per pupil than any other state, according to [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/U.S._Census_Bureau "U.S. Census Bureau") statistics.[[279]](#cite_note-283)

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Government")]

Main article: [Government of New York (state)](/wiki/Government_of_New_York_(state) "Government of New York (state)")

See also: [Law of New York (state)](/wiki/Law_of_New_York_(state) "Law of New York (state)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/NYSCapitolPanorama.jpg/250px-NYSCapitolPanorama.jpg)](/wiki/File:NYSCapitolPanorama.jpg)

The [New York State Capitol](/wiki/New_York_State_Capitol "New York State Capitol") in Albany

The Government of New York embodies the governmental structure of the State of New York as established by the [New York State Constitution](/wiki/New_York_State_Constitution "New York State Constitution"). It is composed of three branches: [executive](/wiki/Executive_branch "Executive branch"), [legislative](/wiki/Legislative_branch "Legislative branch"), and [judicial](/wiki/Judicial_branch "Judicial branch").[[280]](#cite_note-government_branches-284)

The [governor](/wiki/Governor_of_New_York "Governor of New York") is the state's chief executive and is assisted by the [lieutenant governor](/wiki/Lieutenant_Governor_of_New_York "Lieutenant Governor of New York"). Both are elected on the same ticket. Additional elected officers include the [attorney general](/wiki/New_York_Attorney_General "New York Attorney General") and the [comptroller](/wiki/New_York_State_Comptroller "New York State Comptroller"). The [secretary of state](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_of_New_York "Secretary of State of New York"), formerly an elected officer, is currently appointed by the governor.[[281]](#cite_note-285)

The [New York State Legislature](/wiki/New_York_State_Legislature "New York State Legislature") is [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameral "Bicameral") and consists of the [New York State Senate](/wiki/New_York_State_Senate "New York State Senate") and the [New York State Assembly](/wiki/New_York_State_Assembly "New York State Assembly"). The state assembly consists of 150 members, while the state senate varies in its number of members, currently having 63. The legislature is empowered to make laws, subject to the governor's power to [veto](/wiki/Veto "Veto") a bill. However, the veto may be overridden by the legislature if there is a [two-thirds majority](/wiki/Two-thirds_majority "Two-thirds majority") in favor of overriding in each house. The permanent laws of a general nature are [codified](/wiki/Codification_(law) "Codification (law)") in the *[Consolidated Laws of New York](/wiki/Consolidated_Laws_of_New_York "Consolidated Laws of New York")*.[[280]](#cite_note-government_branches-284)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/NYSCourtofAppeals1.JPG/250px-NYSCourtofAppeals1.JPG)](/wiki/File:NYSCourtofAppeals1.JPG)

The [New York Court of Appeals](/wiki/New_York_Court_of_Appeals "New York Court of Appeals") is the highest court of the [New York judiciary](/wiki/Judiciary_of_New_York_(state) "Judiciary of New York (state)")

The highest court of appeal in the [Unified Court System](/wiki/Judiciary_of_New_York "Judiciary of New York") is the [Court of Appeals](/wiki/New_York_State_Court_of_Appeals "New York State Court of Appeals") whereas the primary [felony](/wiki/Felony "Felony") [trial court](/wiki/Trial_court "Trial court") is the [County Court](/wiki/New_York_County_Court "New York County Court") (or the [Supreme Court](/wiki/New_York_Supreme_Court "New York Supreme Court") in New York City). The New York Supreme Court also acts as the intermediate appellate court for many cases, and the local courts handle a variety of other matters including [small claims](/wiki/Small_claims "Small claims"), traffic ticket cases, and local [zoning](/wiki/Zoning "Zoning") matters, and are the starting point for all criminal cases.

The [state is divided](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_New_York "Administrative divisions of New York") into counties, cities, towns, and villages, all of which are [municipal corporations](/wiki/Municipal_corporation "Municipal corporation") with respect to their own governments, as well as various corporate entities that serve single purposes that are also local governments, such as school districts, fire districts, and [New York state public-benefit corporations](/wiki/New_York_state_public-benefit_corporations "New York state public-benefit corporations"), frequently known as *authorities* or *development corporations*. Each municipal corporation is granted varying [home rule](/wiki/Home_rule "Home rule") powers as provided by the New York Constitution. The state also has 10 [Indian reservations](/wiki/Indian_reservation "Indian reservation"). There have been several movements regarding [secession from the state of New York](/wiki/Partition_and_secession_in_New_York "Partition and secession in New York"). Proposals have included a state of [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island"), consisting of everything on the island outside New York City; a state called Niagara, the [western counties](/wiki/Western_New_York "Western New York") of the state of New York; the northern counties of the state of New York called [Upstate New York](/wiki/Upstate_New_York "Upstate New York"); making the city of New York a state; a proposal for a new [Peconic County](/wiki/Peconic_County "Peconic County") on eastern Long Island; and for the borough of [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island") to secede from New York City.[[282]](#cite_note-286)[[283]](#cite_note-287)

In a 2020 study, New York was ranked as the 17th easiest state for citizens to vote in.[[284]](#cite_note-288)

New York's government released a new seal, coat of arms, and flag in April 2020, adding "*[E pluribus unum](/wiki/E_pluribus_unum "E pluribus unum")*" below the state's motto.[[285]](#cite_note-289)[[286]](#cite_note-290) A bill utilizing newly designed flag, arms and seal went into effect in September.[[287]](#cite_note-291)

Revived in the early 2000s, [Abolition Commemoration Day](/wiki/Abolition_Commemoration_Day "Abolition Commemoration Day"), also known as the Fifth of July, is a historic celebration commemorating the abolishment of slavery in New York. In July 2020, the [New York State Assembly](/wiki/New_York_State_Assembly "New York State Assembly") passed legislation officially recognizing Abolition Commemoration Day and [Juneteenth](/wiki/Juneteenth "Juneteenth") in New York.[[288]](#cite_note-nyassembly-292)[[289]](#cite_note-Levine-2014-293) Abolition Commemoration Day is observed on the second Monday in July and Juneteenth on June 19.[[288]](#cite_note-nyassembly-292)

### Federal representation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Federal representation")]

See also: [New York's congressional delegations](/wiki/New_York%27s_congressional_delegations "New York's congressional delegations") and [New York's congressional districts](/wiki/New_York%27s_congressional_districts "New York's congressional districts")

As of 2026, New York contains [twenty-six congressional districts](/wiki/New_York%27s_congressional_districts "New York's congressional districts").[[290]](#cite_note-294)[[291]](#cite_note-295) New York lost a congressional district in 2023 due to the state's slower overall population growth relative to the overall national population growth.[[292]](#cite_note-296) As of 2024, New York has 28 [electoral votes](/wiki/Electoral_College_(United_States) "Electoral College (United States)") in U.S. presidential elections.[[293]](#cite_note-297)

## Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Politics")]

Main article: [Politics of New York (state)](/wiki/Politics_of_New_York_(state) "Politics of New York (state)")

See also: [Elections in New York (state)](/wiki/Elections_in_New_York_(state) "Elections in New York (state)") and [Political party strength in New York (state)](/wiki/Political_party_strength_in_New_York_(state) "Political party strength in New York (state)")

The State of New York is a blue state, or a state where Democratic Party candidates typically win elections.[[294]](#cite_note-298)

[![Kirsten Gillibrand and Chuck Schumer are seen giving a speech promoting universal healthcare.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Chuck_Schumer_and_Kirstin_Gillibrand_June_2009.jpg/250px-Chuck_Schumer_and_Kirstin_Gillibrand_June_2009.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chuck_Schumer_and_Kirstin_Gillibrand_June_2009.jpg)

[Kirsten Gillibrand](/wiki/Kirsten_Gillibrand "Kirsten Gillibrand") and [Chuck Schumer](/wiki/Chuck_Schumer "Chuck Schumer"), the state's two [U.S. Senators](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate") as of 2024

As of 2025, there are more than twice as many enrolled Democratic voters as there are enrolled Republican voters in New York.[[295]](#cite_note-299) As of 2024, no Republican candidate had won a presidential election in New York since [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan") did so in 1984.[[296]](#cite_note-300) As of 2025, no Republican candidate had won a statewide election in New York since [George Pataki](/wiki/George_Pataki "George Pataki") was re-elected governor in 2002.[[297]](#cite_note-301)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Kathy_Hochul%2C_November_2017.jpeg/250px-Kathy_Hochul%2C_November_2017.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Kathy_Hochul,_November_2017.jpeg)

[Kathy Hochul](/wiki/Kathy_Hochul "Kathy Hochul") [(D)](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(New_York) "Democratic Party (New York)"), the [57th](/wiki/List_of_Governors_of_New_York "List of Governors of New York") [Governor of New York](/wiki/Governor_of_New_York "Governor of New York")

As of 2026, New York is represented by [Chuck Schumer](/wiki/Chuck_Schumer "Chuck Schumer") and [Kirsten Gillibrand](/wiki/Kirsten_Gillibrand "Kirsten Gillibrand") in the [United States Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate")[[298]](#cite_note-302)[[299]](#cite_note-303) and is represented by 19 Democrats and seven Republicans in the U.S. House of Representatives.[[300]](#cite_note-304)[[301]](#cite_note-305)

As of 2026, Democrat [Kathy Hochul](/wiki/Kathy_Hochul "Kathy Hochul") is the governor of New York, having served in that capacity since 2021;[[302]](#cite_note-306) the New York State Senate has been led by the Democratic Party since 2019,[[303]](#cite_note-307)[[304]](#cite_note-308) and the New York State Assembly has been led by the Democrats since 1975.[[305]](#cite_note-309)[[306]](#cite_note-310)[[307]](#cite_note-311)

New York City is an important center for [international diplomacy](/wiki/International_diplomacy "International diplomacy").[[308]](#cite_note-312) The [United Nations headquarters](/wiki/Headquarters_of_the_United_Nations "Headquarters of the United Nations") has been situated on the [East Side of Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_East "Midtown East") since 1952.

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Sports")]

Main article: [Sports in New York (state)](/wiki/Sports_in_New_York_(state) "Sports in New York (state)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/The_view_from_the_Grandstand_Level_at_New_Yankee_Stadium.jpg/250px-The_view_from_the_Grandstand_Level_at_New_Yankee_Stadium.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_view_from_the_Grandstand_Level_at_New_Yankee_Stadium.jpg)

[Yankee Stadium](/wiki/Yankee_Stadium "Yankee Stadium") in [The Bronx](/wiki/The_Bronx "The Bronx")

The State of New York is represented by three [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") teams, but is geographically home to one team, the [Buffalo Bills](/wiki/Buffalo_Bills "Buffalo Bills"), based in the [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York") suburb of [Orchard Park](/wiki/Orchard_Park_(town),_New_York "Orchard Park (town), New York"). The other two NFL teams representing New York, the [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants "New York Giants") and [New York Jets](/wiki/New_York_Jets "New York Jets"), also represent the [New York City metropolitan area](/wiki/New_York_metropolitan_area "New York metropolitan area") and were previously located in New York City, but they play in [MetLife Stadium](/wiki/MetLife_Stadium "MetLife Stadium"), located in [East Rutherford, New Jersey](/wiki/East_Rutherford,_New_Jersey "East Rutherford, New Jersey"), about seven miles west of New York City. New York also has two [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") teams, the [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees") (based in [the Bronx](/wiki/The_Bronx "The Bronx")) and the [New York Mets](/wiki/New_York_Mets "New York Mets") (based in [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens")). Minor league baseball teams also play in the State of New York, including the [Long Island Ducks](/wiki/Long_Island_Ducks "Long Island Ducks"), the [Staten Island FerryHawks](/wiki/Staten_Island_FerryHawks "Staten Island FerryHawks"), and the [Brooklyn Cyclones](/wiki/Brooklyn_Cyclones "Brooklyn Cyclones"), downstate, and the [Rochester Red Wings](/wiki/Rochester_Red_Wings "Rochester Red Wings"), the [Binghamton Rumble Ponies](/wiki/Binghamton_Rumble_Ponies "Binghamton Rumble Ponies"), the [Syracuse Mets](/wiki/Syracuse_Mets "Syracuse Mets"), the [Auburn Doubledays](/wiki/Auburn_Doubledays "Auburn Doubledays"), the [Batavia Muckdogs](/wiki/Batavia_Muckdogs "Batavia Muckdogs"), the [Hudson Valley Renegades](/wiki/Hudson_Valley_Renegades "Hudson Valley Renegades") and the [Buffalo Bisons](/wiki/Buffalo_Bisons "Buffalo Bisons") upstate.

New York is home to three [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") franchises: the [New York Rangers](/wiki/New_York_Rangers "New York Rangers") in [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan"), the [New York Islanders](/wiki/New_York_Islanders "New York Islanders") in Nassau County on Long Island, and the [Buffalo Sabres](/wiki/Buffalo_Sabres "Buffalo Sabres") in Buffalo. New York is home to three [American Hockey League](/wiki/American_Hockey_League "American Hockey League") franchises: the [Rochester Americans](/wiki/Rochester_Americans "Rochester Americans"), the [Utica Comets](/wiki/Utica_Comets "Utica Comets"), and the [Syracuse Crunch](/wiki/Syracuse_Crunch "Syracuse Crunch"). The [Adirondack Thunder](/wiki/Adirondack_Thunder "Adirondack Thunder") are the only [ECHL](/wiki/ECHL "ECHL") franchise in New York. New York has two [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") teams, the [New York Knicks](/wiki/New_York_Knicks "New York Knicks") in Manhattan, and the [Brooklyn Nets](/wiki/Brooklyn_Nets "Brooklyn Nets") in [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn") and a [Women's National Basketball Association](/wiki/Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association "Women's National Basketball Association") team, the [New York Liberty](/wiki/New_York_Liberty "New York Liberty"), also based in Brooklyn. New York is the home of a [Major League Soccer](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer "Major League Soccer") franchise, [New York City FC](/wiki/New_York_City_FC "New York City FC"), currently playing in the Bronx, though they are building a new [stadium](/wiki/Etihad_Park_(New_York_City) "Etihad Park (New York City)") in Queens. Although the [New York Red Bulls](/wiki/New_York_Red_Bulls "New York Red Bulls") represent the New York City metropolitan area, they play in [Sports Illustrated Stadium](/wiki/Sports_Illustrated_Stadium "Sports Illustrated Stadium") in [Harrison, New Jersey](/wiki/Harrison,_New_Jersey "Harrison, New Jersey").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Ralph_Wilson_Stadium.jpg/250px-Ralph_Wilson_Stadium.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ralph_Wilson_Stadium.jpg)

[Highmark Stadium](/wiki/Ralph_Wilson_Stadium "Ralph Wilson Stadium") in [Orchard Park](/wiki/Orchard_Park_(town),_New_York "Orchard Park (town), New York")

New York hosted the [1932](/wiki/1932_Winter_Olympics "1932 Winter Olympics") and [1980 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1980_Winter_Olympics "1980 Winter Olympics") at [Lake Placid](/wiki/Lake_Placid,_New_York "Lake Placid, New York"). The 1980 Games are known for the USA–USSR [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") match dubbed the "[Miracle on Ice](/wiki/Miracle_on_Ice "Miracle on Ice")", in which a group of American college students and amateurs defeated the heavily favored Soviet national ice hockey team 4–3 and went on to win the gold medal against [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland"). Along with [St. Moritz](/wiki/St._Moritz "St. Moritz"), [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") and [Innsbruck](/wiki/Innsbruck "Innsbruck"), [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria"), Lake Placid is one of the three cities to have hosted the Winter Olympic Games twice. New York City [bid](/wiki/New_York_City_bid_for_the_2012_Summer_Olympics "New York City bid for the 2012 Summer Olympics") for the [2012 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics "2012 Summer Olympics") but lost to [London](/wiki/London "London"). The annual [United States Open Tennis Championships](/wiki/US_Open_(tennis) "US Open (tennis)") is one of the world's four [Grand Slam](/wiki/Grand_Slam_(tennis) "Grand Slam (tennis)") tennis tournaments and is held at the [National Tennis Center](/wiki/USTA_National_Tennis_Center "USTA National Tennis Center") in [Flushing Meadows–Corona Park](/wiki/Flushing_Meadows%E2%80%93Corona_Park "Flushing Meadows–Corona Park") in Queens.[[309]](#cite_note-313) The [Belmont Stakes](/wiki/Belmont_Stakes "Belmont Stakes"), part of the [Triple Crown of Thoroughbred Racing](/wiki/Triple_Crown_of_Thoroughbred_Racing_(United_States) "Triple Crown of Thoroughbred Racing (United States)"), is held at [Belmont Park](/wiki/Belmont_Park "Belmont Park") in Nassau County on Long Island.

Several U.S. national sports halls of fame are or have been situated in New York. The [National Baseball Hall of Fame and Museum](/wiki/National_Baseball_Hall_of_Fame_and_Museum "National Baseball Hall of Fame and Museum") is located in [Cooperstown](/wiki/Cooperstown "Cooperstown"), [Otsego County](/wiki/Otsego_County,_New_York "Otsego County, New York"). The [National Museum of Racing and Hall of Fame](/wiki/National_Museum_of_Racing_and_Hall_of_Fame "National Museum of Racing and Hall of Fame") in [Saratoga Springs](/wiki/Saratoga_Springs,_New_York "Saratoga Springs, New York"), [Saratoga County](/wiki/Saratoga_County,_New_York "Saratoga County, New York"), honors achievements in the sport of [thoroughbred horse racing](/wiki/Thoroughbred_horse_racing "Thoroughbred horse racing"). The physical facility of the [National Soccer Hall of Fame](/wiki/National_Soccer_Hall_of_Fame "National Soccer Hall of Fame") in [Oneonta](/wiki/Oneonta,_New_York "Oneonta, New York"), also in Otsego County, closed in 2010, although the organization itself has continued inductions.

The state of New York is also home to many intercollegiate division 1 sports programs. The [State University of New York](/wiki/State_University_of_New_York "State University of New York")'s flagship [University at Buffalo](/wiki/University_at_Buffalo "University at Buffalo") are the [Buffalo Bulls](/wiki/Buffalo_Bulls "Buffalo Bulls"). [Syracuse University](/wiki/Syracuse_University "Syracuse University")'s intercollegiate teams are the [Syracuse Orange](/wiki/Syracuse_Orange "Syracuse Orange").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Baseball_HOF_Outside_%282023%29.jpg/250px-Baseball_HOF_Outside_%282023%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Baseball_HOF_Outside_(2023).jpg)

The [National Baseball Hall of Fame](/wiki/National_Baseball_Hall_of_Fame "National Baseball Hall of Fame") in Cooperstown, NY

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(state)&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-NAVD88_10-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-NAVD88_10-1) Elevation adjusted to [North American Vertical Datum of 1988](/wiki/North_American_Vertical_Datum_of_1988 "North American Vertical Datum of 1988")
2. **[^](#cite_ref-11)** To distinguish it from its largest city, [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City")
3. **[^](#cite_ref-12)** The New England states bordering New York are [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut"), [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts"), and [Vermont](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont"). It also shares a [maritime border](/wiki/Maritime_border "Maritime border") with [Rhode Island](/wiki/Rhode_Island "Rhode Island"). The Canadian province of [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec") is to the north, and [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario") to the northwest. [Lake Ontario](/wiki/Lake_Ontario "Lake Ontario") is to the north and [Lake Erie](/wiki/Lake_Erie "Lake Erie") to the west. The [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") touches [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island") in the extreme southeast part of the state.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-161)** Persons of Hispanic or Latino origin are not distinguished between total and partial ancestry.
